note.en: Back cover of the 2024 exhibition catalog

excerpt.en: A quietly forceful Central Xizang Buddha of restrained elegance.

viewingNote.zh: 这是一尊来自西藏中部的佛造像 。乍看之下，祂似乎与其他很多古代造像 无异，但每次凝视 ，内心总被一种无法言说的情绪悄然触动——像雾、像 风，是一种朦胧的辽阔感，像一种“在”，却又“无形”的存在 。后来我才慢慢 意识到，那是一种“虚空”的感觉 。 这是一尊受到帕拉风格影响的造像 。高高的发髻、细长上扬的眉眼、嘴角 一抹耐人寻味的笑意，以及飞扬的衣袖，都带着一种流动的帕拉艺术风格。那 是千年前从东南边境吹入雪域的风 ，停留在了这尊造像上 。
viewingNote.en: This is a Buddhist sculpture hailing from Central Tibet. At first glance, it appears indistinguishable from many other ancient statues; yet, with every lingering gaze, my inner self is quietly stirred by an emotion that defies description—like mist, like wind, a sense of nebulous vastness; a presence that is undeniably "there," yet utterly "formless." Only later did I slowly come to realize that this sensation was, in fact, the feeling of "Emptiness." This sculpture bears the distinct influence of the Pala style. Its towering topknot, slender and upward-sweeping brows and eyes, the enigmatic smile playing upon its lips, and its billowing sleeves all embody the fluid aesthetic of Pala art. It is a wind that drifted into the Land of Snows from the southeastern frontiers a thousand years ago—a breeze that has come to rest upon this very statue.

title.zh: Buddhist Art of the Xizang Realm, P.110, fig.12.
title.en: Buddhist Art of the Xizang Realm, p. 110, fig. 12.

excerpt.en: A compact Kathmandu image whose stillness emerges through precision and proportion.

viewingNote.zh: 站在一件雕塑面前，不知道你是否有过这样的体验：在意识纷乱 、情绪噪 杂的时候，目光之间，仅仅一瞥，这件雕塑作品就能穿透混沌，让人大脑放 空、归于平静 ... 那是一个午后，或许是因天气燥热 、或许是琐事堆积，使人心情浮躁不 安。而就在我步入展厅、目光落在这尊造像上的那刻，一种难以描述清楚的力 量悄然发生 。那种感觉不是震撼，而是一种温和的、深入人心的精神气息，让 我从纷乱的意识中抽离，进入一种忘我的观看状态 。我的意识在那一刻并没有 被拉向某个特定的意义，反而是大脑空空如也，情绪也随之沉降，像是忘记了 上一刻也许还在生气，整个人沉入了一种没有烦恼的状态 。 在之后的几天，大脑里不断在回想那一刻被触动的原因。那种感受不是冲 动的，而是一种深入内在、却又难以捕捉的体验 。它不属于理性分析的范 畴，也不只是审美的快感，更像是一种存在的对话——在混乱生活与精神渴望 之间，这件雕塑成为了某种无声的回应体，以一种无为之境，使我有机会重新 照见了自己的内在节奏。 从雕塑角度来说，这是一件尼泊尔 12 世纪有融合帕拉艺术风格的释迦牟 尼造像 。眉弓细长，鼻梁高挺，眼神柔和，嘴角微笑，整个面部情绪带有一种 早期造像特有的沉思和静谧，非常迷人 。身上的衣饰轻薄 、贴体，勾勒出了身 体的线条。从塑造细节来说，无论是从手臂到肩膀再到胸腔的肌肉走向和转 折，塑造写实，而不是概念化的处理，有一种雕塑的美感 。从形象上看，这尊 释迦造像具有一种令人印象深刻的俊朗，很是帅气 。祂就像现实中那个坐在阳 光下、身穿白衬衫的大学男生，干净、清澈、挺拔，是属于年华最美好的阶段 。 这样的形象，带有一种理想化的青春之美 。 释迦一手于胸前结禅定印，一手结触地印，双腿结跏趺座 。腿部修长而开 阔，腿部因弯曲而略显挤压的肌肉，呈现出一种臌胀感与富有弹性的鲜活气 息 。腿部的比例塑造，在视觉上与头部 、肩膀、腰身再到腿部之间构成了稳定 的三角形结构，使整个造像比例舒朗、大气 。这样的构造既体现出神性的庄严 气质，也让整体形象充满了静定的力量。造像下部为仰覆式莲台，宽大的莲瓣 造型简洁有力，具有鲜明的时代特征，与整尊佛像气质和谐统一。 匠人在塑造心中这个理想形象的时候，有考虑到不同角度的观感 。在背部 的塑造，我们可以看到从肩胛骨的起伏，到背部肌肉线条，再到臀部的转折和 层次，轮廓流畅自然，线条变化微妙。即便在整体造型略带概括性的风格 下，依然能看见骨架、肌肉与衣物之间的结构层次 。简约的造像看似容易，实 际是极具挑战的，因为真正的“炫技”，往往隐藏在简约中。 同样，这尊释迦造像的肢体塑造，也令我联想到米开朗基罗的《大卫》，那 种以真实人体为基础，加以理想化的处理方式，使雕塑既具神性之美，也不失 人性的真实与温度 。这尊释迦牟尼像也同样如此——除了庄严神圣的佛性，在 肢体与气质的呈现中，也流露出一种温和的人性美 。 欣赏一件雕塑，就像我们注视一个人 。那种美 、那种情绪，无需言语或反 复确认，它是直接而纯粹地抵达感官与内心的。就像这尊释迦造像，当我第一 眼看到时，祂所传达的情绪是完整而通透的。佛教雕塑的“理想美”，从来都不 是纯形式的炫技，也不只是信仰的情绪，而是来自对生命本质、理想人格的内 在追问，也更是一种关于“最初自我”的记忆 。 就像那个下午，只需一眼对视 ，脚步驻足，整个人就沉入了一种没有烦恼的 状态 。因为我被造像所散发的情绪唤醒了，一种久违的熟悉感袭来 。我忽然 意识到，释迦散发出的青春和理想之美，像是我曾经无数次在人群中、在镜子 里、在某个阳光穿过树影的午后，看到过的神情——属于青春时期那个青 涩、坦白却又明亮的我 。 那个时候，我们懵懂的坐在树下听着卡带机里的“十年”，还不太懂得复杂 世界的运转，却能安心地睡着，以及毫不焦虑地醒来；那个时候，很多的事我 们做不好，说不清，但却不妨碍我们活得真诚 、有趣 、相信未来 。那种生活的 节奏——慢、松、干净、充满余地 。现在我们，早已被成年人的生活节奏替代 。 而那刻，我竟在一尊造像前又重体验到了那种节奏的气息 。祂的“静”是一种 温柔的照见，让我看见那个曾经单纯 、心中有光的自己；那个没有被烦恼层层 裹住、还愿意相信温柔与秩序的青年。 当然，被造像所唤醒的怀念，并不只是对逝去的眷恋，而是一种更深的整 合、一种温柔的提醒——在被现实打磨之后，我们也能用更成熟的方式，靠近 那个理想中的自己 。
viewingNote.en: Standing before a sculpture, have you ever had this experience? Amidst a chaotic mind and a clamor of emotions—with just a single glance—the artwork cuts through the confusion, allowing the mind to go blank and find a sense of calm... It was an afternoon—perhaps due to the sweltering heat, or perhaps the accumulation of trivial matters—that left me feeling restless and agitated. Yet, the very moment I stepped into the exhibition hall and my gaze fell upon this figure, an indescribable force quietly took hold. It was not a sensation of shock or awe, but rather a gentle, deeply penetrating spiritual presence that drew me away from my mental turmoil and into a state of selfless contemplation. In that moment, my consciousness was not pulled toward any specific meaning; instead, my mind went utterly blank. My emotions settled, as if I had forgotten that just moments before I might have been angry; my entire being sank into a state utterly devoid of worry. In the days that followed, my mind kept returning to that moment, pondering the source of that profound resonance. It was not a fleeting, impulsive sensation, but rather a deeply internal—yet elusive—experience. It transcended the realm of rational analysis and was more than mere aesthetic pleasure; it felt more like an existential dialogue. Bridging the gap between the chaos of daily life and my spiritual yearnings, this sculpture served as a silent respondent—a vessel of "non-doing" that granted me the opportunity to rediscover my own inner rhythm. From a sculptural perspective, this is a 12th-century Nepalese depiction of Shakyamuni Buddha, exhibiting a stylistic fusion with the Pala art tradition. The brow arches are slender, the bridge of the nose high and prominent, the gaze gentle, and the corners of the mouth curved in a subtle smile; the entire facial expression conveys a sense of contemplative serenity and stillness characteristic of early Buddhist iconography—a truly captivating quality. The drapery is rendered as light and clinging, gracefully delineating the contours of the body beneath. In terms of sculptural technique—from the musculature and anatomical transitions flowing from the arms to the shoulders and across the chest—the execution is remarkably realistic rather than merely conceptual, embodying a distinct sculptural beauty. Visually, this figure of Shakyamuni possesses an impressively handsome quality—a striking handsomeness that is truly captivating. He resembles a young university student in real life—sitting in the sunlight, clad in a crisp white shirt—radiating a sense of purity, clarity, and upright vitality; he embodies the very essence of youth in its most beautiful prime. This figure embodies an idealized beauty of youth. Shakyamuni holds one hand at his chest in the Dhyana Mudra (Meditation Gesture) and the other in the Bhumisparsha Mudra (Earth-Touching Gesture), while his legs are crossed in the full lotus position. His limbs are long and expansive; the muscles of his legs—slightly compressed by their bent posture—convey a sense of fullness, volume, and vibrant, elastic vitality. Visually, the proportions of the legs establish a stable triangular structure in relation to the head, shoulders, and torso, endowing the entire sculpture with a sense of spaciousness and grandeur. This structural composition not only manifests a majestic, divine presence but also imbues the figure with a profound, tranquil power. The base of the sculpture consists of an inverted and upright lotus pedestal; the broad lotus petals, rendered with simple yet forceful lines, bear distinct stylistic hallmarks of their era and harmonize perfectly with the overall temperament of the Buddha statue. In crafting this idealized vision, the artisan clearly took into account how the figure would be perceived from various angles. In the rendering of the back, one can observe the subtle undulations of the shoulder blades, the contours of the back muscles, and the nuanced transitions and layering of the buttocks—all defined by fluid, natural outlines and subtle shifts in line work. Even within a stylistic approach that is somewhat generalized in its overall form, the structural interplay between skeleton, musculature, and drapery remains clearly discernible. Creating a minimalist sculpture may appear simple, but it is, in reality, an immense challenge; for true technical virtuosity often lies hidden within simplicity. Similarly, the anatomical rendering of this Shakyamuni figure reminds me of Michelangelo’s *David*—a work grounded in the study of the actual human form yet elevated through an idealized aesthetic treatment, resulting in a sculpture that possesses both divine beauty and an authentic, human warmth. This statue of Shakyamuni achieves precisely the same effect: beyond its solemn and sacred Buddhahood, its physical form and demeanor radiate a gentle, humanistic beauty. To appreciate a sculpture is akin to gazing upon another human being. That beauty—that emotion—requires neither words nor repeated verification; it reaches the senses and the heart directly and purely. Such is the case with this statue of Shakyamuni: from the very first moment I beheld it, the emotion it conveyed felt utterly complete and transparent. The "ideal beauty" embodied in Buddhist sculpture is never merely a display of formal virtuosity, nor is it simply an expression of religious sentiment; rather, it stems from an introspective inquiry into the essence of life and the nature of the ideal human character—and, even more profoundly, it serves as a memory of the "original self." It was just like that afternoon: with a single glance—a moment of eye contact that brought my steps to a halt—my entire being sank into a state utterly free of worldly cares. For I had been awakened by the emotional resonance radiating from the statue; a sense of familiarity, long forgotten, suddenly washed over me. I realized, with sudden clarity, that the beauty of youth and idealism exuded by the figure of Shakyamuni resembled an expression I had encountered countless times before—amidst crowds, in my own reflection, or on some sun-dappled afternoon where light filtered through the trees—an expression belonging to that younger self: that earnest, candid, yet radiant youth I once was. Back then, we would sit beneath the trees in our naivety, listening to the song "Ten Years" on a cassette player; we did not yet grasp the complex workings of the world, yet we could fall asleep with perfect peace of mind and wake without a trace of anxiety. In those days, there were many things we could not do well, nor articulate clearly, yet this did not hinder us from living with sincerity, finding joy in life, and maintaining our faith in the future. It was a rhythm of life—slow, relaxed, pure, and full of breathing room. Now, however, that rhythm has long since been supplanted by the pace of adult life. Yet in that moment, standing before that statue, I once again experienced the faint echo of that very rhythm. Its "stillness" served as a gentle illumination, allowing me to glimpse that former self—the one who was simple-hearted and carried a light within; the young person who had not yet been swaddled layer upon layer in worldly troubles, and who still possessed the willingness to believe in the virtues of gentleness and order. Of course, the nostalgia awakened by the statue is not merely a sentimental longing for what has passed; rather, it represents a deeper form of integration—a gentle reminder that, even after being weathered and refined by the realities of life, we can still find ways—now with greater maturity—to draw closer to that ideal self we aspire to be.

excerpt.en: A standing Vajrapani of upright energy and sharply focused form.

viewingNote.zh: 每次去到巴黎，逛吉美博物馆总是必不可少的行程之一，尽管已经去过很 多次了。每每逛到那几尊喜欢的早期雕塑面前时，我总会停留上好一会儿，那 刻的感受是时间变慢了。整个博物馆仿佛只剩下我与祂之间的静默对视 。我的 思绪被吸引、包裹，心里也逐渐变得安静而满足 。为什么一尊雕塑，会让人的 感知发生如此深刻的变化？祂们静静地站立，不说话、不解释，也不讨好任何 观者的目光 。后知后觉，因为祂们存在于一种比语言更广阔的时间里，那一刻 仿佛不是我在看祂，而是我在通过祂看见自己 。凝视间，疗愈静静发生 ... 第一次看到这件尼泊尔 9-10 世纪的金刚手立像时，接连看了好几天，那种 被吸引的情绪如同逛吉美 。看着这件雕塑我会感觉祂丰富得像一个人，没有任 何单调感，总是给予人一种美好的感受，充满耐看性 。 不同地域的造像，总是悄然携带着它所诞生之地的历史背景与文化记忆 。 李查维王朝是尼泊尔历史上第一个有明确文献记载的王朝，主要势力集中于加 德满都谷地 。由于地理相邻 、文化交汇以及族群联系等多重因素，印度笈多王 朝造像中典雅、内省、充满精神性的艺术风格，对早期尼泊尔李查维时期的造 像产生了深远的影响 。在这尊金刚手菩萨的面容上，我们仍能感受到那种来自 古典时代的纯净气息 。那如呼吸般自然的微笑，仿佛只要多看一眼，心中的浮 躁就能被安抚 。菩萨头戴三叶冠，耳后冠结处点缀着细致的花朵装饰，头顶 发髻高束，如一朵圆形莲蓬；垂肩而下的发丝前方，佩戴着具有明显身份和时 代印记的圆形与花瓣形耳环。 这尊金刚手与其他菩萨造像有所不同的是，大多数时候，我们看到的菩萨 眼神是温柔的、慈悲的，是在凝视着某一个具体的人，回应他的祈愿；而这尊 却相反 。当我试图捕捉祂的眼神时，首先发现的是：在祂的眼里是有着丰富内 容的——那是一种既写实又超然的深度，像是一扇半掩的门，门后通向一个更 辽阔的意识空间。其次，祂的眼神并不在于你，甚至也不是在看任何一个具体 的人 。祂的目光似乎越过了现实与肉身，落在一个遥远而无形的地方。祂如一 位穿越时间的“沉思者”，将万物收于一念之中。那种目光不带评判，也无悲 喜，反而像是在凝望一个永恒的问题，而答案，也并不急着说出，只是缓缓浮 现于那静默之间。这种神韵的无限性所给观者带来的思索和反复回味，也正是 成就了造像耐看性的一个重要因素。 最初第一眼见到这尊金刚手时，首先抓住我眼球并让我开始思索的其实是 祂那带有显著动势的三折姿。因为乍看之下，这样幅度较大的躯干扭动，似乎 会带来某种不稳定感，但令人惊喜的是，细看之下，而祂却呈现出了极强的稳定 性与内在平衡。这种矛盾般的观感，显然是来自匠人对身体结构与视觉张力的 深刻理解与再次创造 。造像整体衣纹线条简练，几乎剔除了冗余的装饰性褶 皱，仅以最基础的线性来勾勒出形体的起伏 。而在身体的块面处理上，也延续 了早期简约的风格，留白与概括并用，使形体在塑造中更具耐看性 。 菩萨肩部宽广、挺阔，一手高抬呈无畏印，给予安心；一手手持象征身份 的金刚杵自然下垂，表征无坚不摧的“金刚之心”。如果我们尝试以造像最中间 的帽冠顶部作为一个点，顺着身体中心绘出一条垂直的中轴线 ，我们就会清晰 的看见匠人在体态塑造方面的创新。右侧上半身形体在空间中的 C 字形运动 轨迹从腋下延展至腰部，再过渡至髋部，形成一个向内扭转的连贯曲线 ，如风 中之枝；下半身腿部则是近乎静止，如一根笔直的立柱般顺延至脚掌垂直而 立，如山岩不动；而左侧上半身没有动态，下半身腿部则是以人体最舒适的姿 势站立，松弛且自然 。于视觉上，左右两侧在动与静之间形成了鲜明的对 照——这正是三折姿之所以“动中有稳”的美学根基 。 可以说，匠人在创作时，并没有过多参照自然人体的动作，而是一种基于真 实生活观察和自己理解之后的“理想化再创作”。匠人并没有拘泥于现实形体 的被动模仿，而是在掌握动态平衡与视觉节奏的基础上，对人体进行了自己的 重构。动静交融之间，不仅凸显了造像的空间张力，也赋予祂一种近乎音乐般 的节奏感，使观者在视觉能捕捉到一种“如行云流水般流动，又如山岳般沉稳” 的双重体验。而这种双重体验也正是成就这尊造像耐看性的另一个重要因 素，也是匠人的高明之处——在看似矛盾中创造和谐，在有限的空间中唤起无 限的生命感 。 我们常说“工艺”决定了外形，但“意蕴”成就了灵魂 。技艺可以雕刻出形，但 意蕴往往来自于匠人的信念、愿力、境界，乃至那个时代的共情。10 世纪的尼 泊尔，随着李查维王朝的结束而进入了混乱的过渡时期，就在那样的时代缝隙 中，这尊金刚手的悄然诞生，似乎并非偶然，应是“另有深意”。祂更像是一种 回应和指引，金刚手眼神中所散发出的美好与希望，也许正是匠人、供养人在 用自己的方式，想把虔诚的观者指引去祂所指的那个方向——平和、自由、超 越 、宽广… 或许，这也是一则关于“抵达”的故事。 那么，你呢？ 你是否，也已经注视中，开始疗愈、开始悄然靠近了那份安定与辽阔？
viewingNote.en: Whenever I visit Paris, a stroll through the Guimet Museum is always an indispensable part of my itinerary—even though I have been there countless times. Whenever I find myself standing before those few early sculptures I particularly cherish, I invariably linger for quite a while; in those moments, it feels as though time itself has slowed down. The entire museum seems to fade away, leaving only a silent, mutual gaze shared between the sculpture and me. My thoughts are drawn in and enveloped, and my heart gradually settles into a state of quiet contentment. Why is it that a mere sculpture can trigger such a profound shift in one’s perception? They stand in silence—speaking no words, offering no explanations, and making no effort to court the gaze of any viewer. Only in retrospect did I realize the reason: because they exist within a temporal realm far vaster than that of language. In those moments, it feels less like I am looking *at* the sculpture, and more like I am looking *through* it to see myself. Amidst that silent gaze, healing quietly takes place... When I first encountered this standing figure of Vajrapani—a Nepalese sculpture dating from the 9th or 10th century—I found myself returning to view it day after day; the sense of magnetic attraction I felt was akin to my experiences wandering through the Guimet Museum. As I gaze upon this sculpture, I sense a richness within it—as multifaceted as a living human being—devoid of any monotony, consistently evoking a sense of beauty and possessing an enduring visual depth that rewards repeated contemplation. Sculptural works from different regions invariably carry, in a subtle yet profound manner, the historical context and cultural memory of the lands in which they were born. The Licchavi Dynasty was the first in Nepalese history to be substantiated by clear documentary records, with its primary sphere of influence centered within the Kathmandu Valley. Due to a confluence of factors—including geographical proximity, cultural exchange, and ethnic ties—the artistic style of India’s Gupta Empire (characterized by its elegance, introspection, and profound spirituality) exerted a deep and lasting influence on the early Licchavi-period sculptures of Nepal. In the countenance of this Vajrapani Bodhisattva, we can still discern that pure, ethereal aura emanating from the classical era. His smile—as natural and effortless as a breath—seems to possess the power to soothe the restlessness of the heart with a mere glance. The Bodhisattva wears a tripartite crown; the knots of the crown behind his ears are adorned with intricate floral motifs, while his hair is gathered into a high topknot resembling a spherical lotus pod. Framing the strands of hair that cascade over his shoulders, he wears circular and petal-shaped earrings—ornaments that bear the unmistakable hallmarks of his specific identity and historical epoch. What distinguishes this statue of Vajrapani from other Bodhisattva images is a striking contrast: while we typically see Bodhisattvas with gentle, compassionate gazes—seemingly fixed upon a specific individual to answer their prayers—this figure presents the exact opposite. When I attempted to capture His gaze, the first thing I discovered was the profound depth contained within His eyes—a depth that is simultaneously realistic and transcendent, akin to a half-open door leading into a vast, expansive realm of consciousness. Furthermore, His gaze is not directed at you, nor is it fixed upon any specific person. His eyes seem to transcend the boundaries of physical reality and the corporeal form, settling instead upon a distant, invisible realm. He resembles a "Thinker" traversing the currents of time, gathering the entirety of creation into a single thought. This gaze carries no judgment, nor does it betray any emotion of joy or sorrow; rather, it appears to be contemplating an eternal question—one for which the answer is not hastily revealed, but allowed to slowly emerge from the depths of that profound silence. The sense of infinite mystery conveyed by this spiritual aura—and the deep contemplation and lingering reflection it inspires in the viewer—is precisely what endows this sculpture with its enduring visual appeal. When I first laid eyes on this Vajrapani, what immediately captured my attention—and sparked my initial contemplation—was His *tribhanga* (triple-bend) posture, characterized by its distinct sense of dynamic motion. At first glance, such a dramatic twisting of the torso might seem to create a sense of instability; yet, surprisingly, upon closer inspection, the figure reveals an extraordinary sense of stability and inner equilibrium. This seemingly paradoxical visual impression is clearly the result of the artisan's profound understanding of anatomical structure and visual tension, and their subsequent creative reinterpretation of these elements. The drapery lines throughout the sculpture are concise and streamlined; almost all superfluous decorative folds have been eliminated, leaving only the most fundamental linear contours to delineate the undulating forms of the body. Similarly, the treatment of the body's planes adheres to an early, minimalist aesthetic—employing a blend of negative space and stylistic generalization—which further enhances the visual richness and enduring appeal of the sculpted form. The Bodhisattva’s shoulders are broad and commanding; one hand is raised high in the *Abhaya Mudra* (Gesture of Fearlessness), offering reassurance and protection, while the other hangs naturally, holding the *Vajra* (thunderbolt scepter)—the symbol of His divine identity—thereby embodying the indestructible "Diamond Heart." If we attempt to trace a vertical central axis—starting from the very apex of the figure's crown and extending downward through the center of the body—we clearly perceive the craftsman's innovative approach to sculpting the human form. On the right side, the upper torso follows a C-shaped trajectory through space, extending from the armpit to the waist before transitioning to the hip; this creates a fluid, inwardly twisting curve that sways like a branch in the wind. The lower body on this side, however, remains nearly motionless; the leg descends vertically like a straight pillar to the foot, standing as steadfast and immovable as mountain rock. Conversely, the left side of the upper torso exhibits no dynamic movement, while the corresponding leg assumes a stance of utmost human comfort—relaxed and entirely natural. Visually, the left and right sides establish a striking contrast between motion and stillness—precisely the aesthetic foundation upon which the *Tribhanga* (triple-bend) posture achieves its characteristic beauty of "stability within motion." It could be argued that in creating this piece, the craftsman did not merely replicate the movements of the natural human body; rather, he engaged in an "idealized re-creation"—one grounded in observations of real life yet filtered through his own unique understanding. The craftsman did not confine himself to a passive imitation of physical reality; instead, having mastered the principles of dynamic equilibrium and visual rhythm, he undertook a personal reconstruction of the human form. Through this fusion of motion and stillness, the sculpture not only accentuates its spatial tension but also imbues the figure with a rhythm akin to music, offering the viewer a dual visual experience: a sense of fluidity "like flowing clouds and water," coupled with a sense of steadfastness "like a towering mountain." This dual experience constitutes another pivotal factor in the sculpture's enduring visual appeal—and therein lies the craftsman's true genius: the ability to forge harmony amidst apparent contradiction, and to evoke a boundless sense of vitality within the confines of finite space. We often say that "craftsmanship" determines form, but "meaning" gives birth to the soul. Technical skill can sculpt the physical shape, yet true meaning invariably stems from the craftsman's beliefs, aspirations, spiritual depth, and even the collective empathy of the era in which he lived. In 10th-century Nepal—a period of chaotic transition following the collapse of the Licchavi dynasty—the quiet emergence of this figure of Vajrapani within such a historical interstice appears to be no mere coincidence; rather, it seems to carry a significance that runs far deeper. It feels less like a mere presence and more like a form of response and guidance. The beauty and hope radiating from Vajrapani’s gaze may well be the very message the artisans and patrons—each in their own way—sought to convey: guiding the devout viewer toward the direction He points to—peace, freedom, transcendence, and expansiveness. Perhaps, too, this is a story about "arrival." And what of you? As you gaze upon Him, have you, too, begun to heal—begun to quietly draw closer to that sense of serenity and vastness?

excerpt.en: A Shalu-style Vajrasattva combining Nepalese refinement with Xizang gravity.

viewingNote.zh: 这尊融合了尼泊尔艺术风格的金刚萨垛造像，带有典型的夏鲁寺艺术特征 。 一听到“金刚”两个字，我们第一反应往往会联想到勇猛、怒目等具有极强张力 的画面。但这尊造像给我们的视觉感受却恰恰相反，祂是“忿怒中的温柔者”。 金刚萨埵这个题材，是藏传佛教中非常重要的本尊之一。“金刚”象征着 不变、不坏 、真实、本觉和空性；“萨埵”则意为友情、心者、勇士。因此，金 刚萨埵这个名字在文化中可直译为：具有金刚之心的觉者，或者是有勇气直面 与转化烦恼的勇士。 在密教传统中，金刚总持是佛陀究竟的法身，是智慧与空性的圆满体现 。 然而，法身超越时空，抽象而无相，不易被众生直接感知和依止 。于是，金刚 总持以种种化身示现，回应不同众生的需要。金刚萨埵便是其中极为关键的一 种化身——专为帮助修行者消除业障 、恢复清净而出现 。 佛教中“化身”的观念，揭示了一个重要的哲学关系：真理若不化为可触、可 用的形式，就无法进入人的生命。金刚总持象征“究竟”，而金刚萨埵则是这种 “究竟”在修行路途中的“实践形式”。换言之：金刚总持是太阳本身；金刚萨 埵是穿过云层，能够温暖人心的光 。 人类的生命不是在无尘的空境里展开，而是在业力、习气和情绪的漩涡中 不断轮转。即使知道有智慧与空性的终极真理，如果心灵没有经过清理与净 化，我们也无法真正承载它。金刚萨埵的意义正在于此：祂不是终点，而是帮 助修行者“抵达终点”的关键力量。通过祂的修持，修行人不断消解业障，使 内心逐渐透明，从而使智慧得以在心中显现 。 在现代语境中，祂代表了人类必须直面并清理内心阴影的过程 。我们每个 人心中都有过往的创伤、未消解的执念与负面循环，若不加以面对，就会形成 “心理业障”，阻碍我们与真实的自我接触 。就像真理从来不是一蹴而就的，而 是通过“去蔽”的过程逐渐显现 。金刚萨埵正是这个“去蔽”的化身，把隐藏 在本体中的光带回到日常生命。 当我第一次看到这尊金刚萨垛造像时，也许是因为融入了尼泊尔风格的原 因，带给我一种极强的“结实”感 。造像从肩膀到膝盖的转折处，如方形的折 来源：Christie’s,New York,Friday,20 March,2009, no.1365 角般有力；肌肉结构臌胀，以方形的块面呈现；肩膀偏方正，肢体厚重，下面 底座稳固大气，整体看上去都是有一种方形的状态 。这种整体的“方形语 言”，给人一种坚实、强壮、有支撑力的力量感 。 尽管形态结实，但与此同时，祂又带着一股仙气 。略微偏转的头部姿 态，手持金刚杵与铃的动势，为端庄的坐姿增添了动感 。面庞圆润，额头饱 满，姿态挺拔，目光略垂的眼部形状秀气 ，且刻线柔和、飘逸，神情间流露出 温和、清朗的“神性”。加上嘴角微扬的笑意所折射出的情绪，让人能感受到祂 内在的清净与喜悦 。 金刚萨垛头戴五叶宝冠、脸型圆中带方、额髻宽广、眼 型像细长的杏 仁、鼻部小巧高挺，这种开脸特征为夏鲁寺造像的典型风格。宽大的耳垂上佩 戴着圆形耳环，身上所戴的臂钏、璎珞和裙边，都以颗颗饱满的连珠纹装 饰；在繁与简之间恰到好处地取得了平衡。底座上的莲瓣清晰明朗、舒展有 度，在底层大颗连珠纹的托举中，有一种呼吸般的张合节奏，使得整尊造像在 自然吐纳之间透着一种大气和高贵。 尽管造像整体呈现的是比较壮硕的感觉，但两侧脑后轻薄的飘带材质，恰 如其分地增添了一种“风”的存在 。这种“轻”与“重”的对比，也让整尊造像 更具灵性与动感了。飘带在脑后舒展，又像是背光的晕环，为整体营造出了一种 更为具象的神圣场域 。凝望间，我仿佛看到祂像是飘在空中，正乘坐一片祥云 飘向而来，从空性中显现，如祂名字所象征的那般纯净、和煦 。 祂就像一道内在觉性的光，悄然降临 。祂不为让你逃避罪与苦，而是提醒 你将它带入光中，那像是一种唤醒——你或许曾迷失、堕落 、错失方向，但你 从未真正失去清净的本性 。如果你愿意承认、转身，那这份勇气将化为作金刚 般真实、坚不可摧的力量 。因为，清净从来不是因修行而得来，而是你本自具 足，你只需要去拨开那些遮蔽它的层层迷雾罢了。
viewingNote.en: This statue of Vajrasattva, which blends Nepalese artistic styles, bears the distinctive artistic characteristics typical of Shalu Monastery. Upon hearing the word "Vajra," our immediate reaction often conjures images of ferocity, glaring eyes, and scenes charged with intense tension. Yet, the visual impression conveyed by this particular statue is precisely the opposite; He is the "Gentle One amidst the Wrathful." The figure of Vajrasattva represents one of the most significant deities within the tradition of Tibetan Buddhism. "Vajra" symbolizes immutability, indestructibility, truth, primordial awareness, and emptiness; "Sattva," meanwhile, denotes friendship, a being of heart, or a warrior. Consequently, within this cultural context, the name Vajrasattva can be literally translated as: "The Awakened One possessing a Vajra-like heart," or "The Warrior with the courage to directly confront and transform afflictions." In the esoteric tradition, Vajradhara represents the Buddha's ultimate Dharmakaya—the perfect embodiment of wisdom and emptiness. However, the Dharmakaya transcends time and space; being abstract and formless, it is not easily perceived or relied upon directly by sentient beings. Thus, Vajradhara manifests through various emanations to respond to the diverse needs of different beings. Vajrasattva constitutes one such pivotal emanation—one that appears specifically to assist practitioners in purifying karmic obstacles and restoring their innate purity. The Buddhist concept of "emanation" reveals a profound philosophical relationship: unless truth is transformed into a tangible and accessible form, it cannot truly enter into human life. Vajradhara symbolizes the "Ultimate Reality," while Vajrasattva serves as the "Practical Form" of this Ultimate Reality along the path of spiritual practice. In other words: Vajradhara is the Sun itself; Vajrasattva is the light that pierces through the clouds to warm the human heart. Human life does not unfold within a pristine, dust-free void, but rather revolves ceaselessly within a vortex of karma, habitual tendencies, and emotions. Even if we possess intellectual knowledge of the ultimate truths of wisdom and emptiness, we cannot truly embody them unless our minds have undergone a process of cleansing and purification. This is precisely where the significance of Vajrasattva lies: He is not the destination itself, but rather the pivotal force that assists practitioners in "reaching that destination." Through the practice associated with Him, the practitioner continuously dissolves karmic obstacles, allowing the inner self to gradually become transparent, thereby enabling wisdom to manifest within the heart. In a modern context, He represents the process by which humanity must directly confront and cleanse its inner shadows. Within each of us reside past traumas, unresolved attachments, and negative cycles; if left unaddressed, these form "psychological karma" that hinders us from connecting with our true selves. Just as truth is never realized overnight—but rather emerges gradually through a process of "unveiling"—Vajrasattva serves as the very embodiment of this "unveiling," bringing the light hidden within our essential nature back into our daily lives. When I first beheld this sculpture of Vajrasattva—perhaps due to the incorporation of Nepalese stylistic elements—it evoked in me a profound sense of "solidity." The transitions in the figure's form, from the shoulders down to the knees, possess a powerful angularity akin to sharp, square folds; the musculature appears taut and swelling, articulated in distinct, block-like planes. With His broad, square-set shoulders, substantial limbs, and a sturdy, imposing base, the figure projects an overall impression of geometric squareness. This pervasive "square aesthetic" conveys a sense of strength that is solid, robust, and deeply supportive. Yet, despite this physical solidity, He simultaneously radiates an ethereal, celestial aura. The slight tilt of His head and the dynamic posture of His hands—holding the *vajra* (thunderbolt scepter) and bell—infuse His dignified seated pose with a sense of movement. His face is rounded, His forehead full and broad, and His posture upright; His eyes, with their slightly downcast gaze and elegant almond shape, are rendered with soft, flowing incised lines, allowing an expression of gentle, luminous "divinity" to shine through. Furthermore, the subtle smile playing upon the corners of His lips reflects an inner state that allows the viewer to perceive His inherent purity and joy. Vajrasattva is depicted wearing a five-lobed jeweled crown; His face is rounded yet possesses a square-jawed structure, His forehead is broad, His eyes resemble slender almonds, and His nose is small yet high-bridged—facial characteristics that exemplify the distinctive stylistic tradition of the Shalu Monastery. Circular earrings adorn the broad earlobes; the armbands, necklaces, and the hems of the robes are all embellished with rows of plump, continuous pearls—achieving a perfect balance between complexity and simplicity. The lotus petals on the base are rendered with crisp clarity and graceful expansiveness; supported by the large, continuous pearls of the lowest tier, they seem to pulse with a rhythmic, breathing motion, imbuing the entire sculpture with an air of grandeur and nobility as it appears to naturally inhale and exhale. Although the sculpture conveys a sense of robust solidity overall, the delicate streamers fluttering behind the head on either side aptly evoke the very presence of the "wind." This interplay between "lightness" and "heaviness" lends the entire figure a heightened sense of spirituality and dynamism. As the streamers unfurl behind the head—resembling the radiant halo of a mandorla—they create a tangible, sacred aura surrounding the figure. As I gaze upon it, I feel as though I see Him floating in mid-air, riding upon an auspicious cloud as He drifts toward me—manifesting from the void of Emptiness, as pure and gentle as the very name He symbolizes. He descends quietly, like a beam of light born of inner awareness. He does not come merely to offer you an escape from sin and suffering, but rather to remind you to bring them into the light; it is a form of awakening—for though you may have once been lost, fallen, or adrift, you have never truly forfeited your pristine, pure nature. If you are willing to acknowledge this truth and turn your path around, that very courage will transform into a power as real and indestructible as a diamond. For purity is never something acquired through spiritual practice; it is a quality you have possessed all along—you need only part the layers of obscuring mists that have veiled it from your sight.

excerpt.en: A Bihar image of Avalokiteshvara shaped by Pala elegance and devotional calm.

viewingNote.zh:
如果要在印度晚期佛教艺术中寻找一个真正的高峰，帕拉王朝几乎无法绕开。

8 世纪中叶，瞿波罗王建立帕拉王朝，其版图大致覆盖今天印度东部的比哈尔邦、

西孟加拉邦以及孟加拉国一带。此后四百余年间，历代君王持续护持佛教，使这

一时期成为印度佛教发展的最后一个黄金时代。菩提迦耶的大菩提寺、那烂陀的

大僧院，都在这一阶段继续扩展和兴盛；而飞行寺、超戒寺等重要寺院的建立，

也使大乘佛教与密教在此并行发展、相互激荡。来自亚洲各地的朝圣僧侣、留学

僧和佛教信徒汇集于此，使帕拉王朝不仅成为佛法传播的中心，也成为佛教造像

艺术最成熟、最精密的生产地之一。也正是在这样的背景下，眼前这尊 11 世纪

的嵌银嵌红铜世尊观音，才显得格外重要。它并不仅仅是一件造像精品，更像是

帕拉王朝成熟期审美、工艺与宗教观念共同凝结出的一个典范性答案。

观世音菩萨在佛教传统中具有三十三种应化身，世尊观世音即其一。此尊造像最

令人惊叹的地方，在于它并不是单凭某一个局部取胜，而是从整体气韵到细部刻

画，都呈现出极高完成度。合金青铜在长久流传中逐渐形成了细润而温厚的表面

质感，使整个造像在金属固有的坚实之外，又生出一种几乎近于肌肤的柔和光泽。

正是这种经过时间沉淀后的温润，使观音本应超越世俗的圣洁感，被转化成了一

种可感、可亲、可被凝视的在场。

若从头部开始细看，这尊观音的身份与时代特征都极为明确。菩萨头戴小三叶冠，

冠缘嵌银，并以连珠纹精细装饰，层次分明而富于秩序。发髻高耸、发丝盘绕，

整体偏扁平，盘束的发辫一股一股清晰可辨，如麻绳般紧密缠绕，显出极强的塑

造控制力。发髻正前方化现一尊阿弥陀佛，这一细节不仅点明了观音的身份，也

明确了其与莲花部的归属关系。耳侧的扇形冠结横向展开，缯带向上翻扬，带有

鲜明的装饰性，却并未破坏整体的庄严，反而在稳定中增添了向上的生气。

再看面部，这尊观音几乎集中体现了成熟帕拉造像最迷人的开脸方式。额间长方

形白毫、嵌银双目、嵌红铜双唇，与近黄铜色的面部肌肤形成了极其精妙的色彩

对比。这种材质上的综合色，并非仅为了华丽，而是在逼真、神圣与装饰之间取

得一种平衡。高挑如弓的双眉末端与鼻梁自然相连，鼻部高挺，嘴唇丰厚而微微

向中间收束，面庞既有南亚人种特征的写实依据，又经过理想化提炼，形成一种

既真实又超越现实的神性容貌。换句话说，这张脸并不只是在“塑造观音”，而是

在回答一个更深的问题：一尊菩萨应当如何既保有人的温度，又显出超越人的光

辉？

身体部分的处理，同样是帕拉造像成熟时期的重要标志。观音双肩宽阔，胸部厚

实，双臂圆润，整个躯体挺拔而不僵硬。匠人并没有依赖夸张的装饰来掩盖形体，

而是通过打磨与收面，直接塑出胸肌、小腹与四肢起伏的轮廓，使人体结构清晰

而自然。这里最值得注意的是：这种自然写实，并不是对现实肉身的被动复制，

而是一种带有古典主义意味的理想化处理。身体既健硕又节制，既有力量又无粗

粝，显示出匠人对“菩萨之身”这一概念的深刻理解——它必须比现实更完整、更

稳定，也更能承载神圣性。

菩萨胸前佩戴单层嵌银项链，下方为嵌宝石璎珞，双臂饰菱形臂钏；胸前巾帛、

圣索、腰带、裙边，则分别以嵌银、嵌红铜与阴线刻画配合完成。这里的高明之

处，在于装饰虽多，却没有一处显得多余。每一种材质都承担着不同的视觉功能：

银使轮廓清亮，红铜使局部生动，而阴线则负责唤起贴体衣料的柔软感。于是，

装饰不再只是附着于身体之上的华美，而成为形体节奏的一部分。

观音左手执莲，右手轻垂膝前施予愿印，这一组合意义非常明确：一边是莲花象

征的清净，一边是愿印所表达的允诺与救度。菩萨并非高踞于不可触及之处，而

是以一种留驻世间、主动垂顾的姿态面对众生。两侧花枝蜿蜒向上，于肩头绽放，

既强化了观音与莲部的关系，又使整尊造像在稳定之中生出一种生命正在伸展的

活力。在以花枝、缯带、手印与目光共同表达一种“向外流动的慈悲”。

下方的仰覆式莲座，同样值得反复观看。上下缘连珠纹颗粒饱满，排列严谨，尤

其底层连珠夸张而丰厚，几乎像一颗颗独立镶嵌而成，形成极强的视觉节奏。莲

瓣满饰一周，平整而舒展，上下错落有致；在莲瓣与莲台之间，又嵌有三圈红铜，

使整个底座在金属色调的对比中更富层次。莲台背面的两行古梵文，字体娟秀而

劲健，进一步说明这不是一件普通作品，而是一件带有明确宗教身份与供养背景

的高规格作品。

如果将这些局部重新收回到整体中，就会发现，这尊世尊观音之所以能成为帕拉

成熟期的典型，并不只是因为它符合若干风格特征，而是因为它把帕拉造像最本

质的几个问题，都处理得非常完整：其一，如何在写实与神圣之间取得平衡；其

二，如何在华丽装饰中维持整体秩序；其三，如何让慈悲不是抽象概念，而是通

过形体、神情与姿态真实显现出来。

也正因为如此，这尊观音的气质并不单一。它有健硕挺拔的躯体，因此显出坚定

与英勇；有高弓般的双眉、微扬的嘴角与微含的下颌，因此又带着一种沉静而深

邃的悲悯；如山高耸的发髻与向上翻动的缯带，使寂静相中又生出肃穆与威仪；

而花枝与蓓蕾的柔软，则像将无边之爱化作甘露，柔软且悄然地洒向世间。诸多

看似不同的力量，被匠人组织成一个整体，于是这尊造像最终呈现出的，便不是

单一情绪，而是一种异乎寻常的寂静力量。所谓“寂静力量”，并不是无声无息，

而是无需夸张张扬，便已具足穿透人心的分量。

所以，当我们站在这尊世尊观音面前时，看到的并不只是一件 11 世纪的青铜造

像。我们真正看到的，是印度佛教最后黄金时代如何把自己的审美、信仰、工艺

与理想人格，压缩进一尊像中；也看到，一位伟大的匠人如何用金属、宝石、线

条与比例，让“慈悲”不再只是被诵读的词语，而成为可以被目光直接感知的形体。

附注：

铜像基座后部的莲瓣上镌刻梵文铭文一则，根据卡锡纳特.塔摩特（Kashinath Tamot）的

释读，该铭文以古俱胝罗字体（Kutila script）的变体书写，应属北印度地域化城体（Nāgar

ī script）的一种，为公元 11 世纪前后所流行。1 内容具体如下：

“देवव_गय_ झांव_लक नकष पू_यपाहवकस ।।”；

罗马字母转写：Devavaggarya Jhāṃmveṃlika nikaṣa pūṇyapālhalokasa ||；

汉译：“庞雅波罗洛迦舍向（圣）观自在菩萨敬顶礼”。

关于铭文中提及的人名“庞雅波罗洛迦舍（pūṇyapālhalokasa）”，其身份与生活年

代暂不可考，但可推测其应为来自印度的一名僧人。尤需关注的是铭文中对观音菩萨的称谓

并未采用梵文传统中的“Avalokiteśvara”，而是借用了藏语称谓“坚惹色”（ སྤाན་ास་གस་ा , Chenrizik），并经对音转写为梵文“Jhāṃmveṃlika”后再行刻写。2 显而易见，此乃充分

考虑到藏语中对于观音菩萨梵文称谓的发音困难而采取的方法，带有强烈的面向藏族传统的

趋同性。在目前经过已经译读的波罗风格造像铭文中，这是十分罕见的案例，仅在西藏林周

县杰拉康所发现的波罗风格石刻中存在类似情况。3 我们亦可由此断定，此尊铜像应是印度

施主专为西藏供奉所需而造。

摘自马美美文章

注释:

1. 此处参照 Kashinath Tamot 所提供的铭文释读结果：An Inscription of a Padmapāṇi

Avalokiteśvara, 5 October 2020. Personal communication. 2. 前揭注释 1。

3. 杰拉康弥勒菩萨石像铭文同样采用藏文转写梵文的方式镌刻，根据夏吾卡先观点，

杰拉康弥勒菩萨石像应为藏族匠师于西藏本地雕制。见夏吾卡先: 《西藏林周县杰拉康帕拉

风格石雕造像与碑刻的调查研究》。成都：四川大学出版社，《藏学学刊》第 22 辑，2020

年第一期。页 8198，图版 45。
viewingNote.en:
If one seeks to identify a true pinnacle within the realm of late Buddhist art in India, the Pala Dynasty is a period that simply cannot be overlooked.

In the mid-8th century, King Gopala established the Pala Dynasty, a realm whose territory roughly encompassed the regions corresponding to present-day Bihar and West Bengal in eastern India, as well as Bangladesh. Over the ensuing four centuries, successive monarchs consistently patronized and protected the Buddhist faith, thereby ushering in what would become the final golden age of Buddhism in India. During this era, major institutions such as the Mahabodhi Temple in Bodh Gaya and the Great Monastery at Nalanda continued to expand and flourish; furthermore, the establishment of pivotal monastic centers—such as Vikramashila and Odantapuri—facilitated the parallel development and dynamic interplay of Mahayana and Vajrayana Buddhism. Pilgrims, visiting scholars, and devotees from across Asia converged upon this region, transforming the Pala realm into not only a central hub for the dissemination of the Dharma but also one of the most sophisticated and refined centers for the production of Buddhist iconography. It is precisely against this historical backdrop that the 11th-century figure before us—a silver- and copper-inlaid depiction of *Bhagavan Avalokiteshvara*—assumes such extraordinary significance. It stands as far more than merely a masterpiece of sculptural art; rather, it serves as an exemplary embodiment—a definitive answer—crystallizing the aesthetic sensibilities, technical craftsmanship, and religious ideals characteristic of the Pala Dynasty’s mature period.

Within the Buddhist tradition, the Bodhisattva Avalokiteshvara is said to manifest in thirty-three different incarnate forms; *Bhagavan Avalokiteshvara* represents one such manifestation. The most astonishing quality of this particular sculpture lies in the fact that its excellence does not derive from any single isolated detail; rather, it demonstrates an exceptional degree of artistic perfection across the entire spectrum—from its overarching spiritual aura (*qiyun*) to the meticulous rendering of its minute details. Through the passage of time and centuries of handling, the bronze alloy has acquired a surface texture that is at once smooth, lustrous, and imbued with a gentle warmth. This quality endows the sculpture with a softness—almost akin to the texture of human skin—that transcends the inherent rigidity of the metal itself. It is precisely this mellow patina—the product of time’s gentle refinement—that transforms Avalokiteshvara’s inherent sanctity (a quality typically perceived as transcending the mundane world) into a palpable, approachable, and intimately present reality—one that invites and sustains our contemplative gaze.

Upon closer examination—beginning with the head—both the specific identity of this Avalokiteshvara figure and the stylistic characteristics of its historical era are revealed with striking clarity. The Bodhisattva wears a small, three-lobed crown.

The rim of the crown is inlaid with silver and exquisitely adorned with a continuous pearl motif—a design characterized by distinct layers and a sense of perfect order. The hair is gathered into a towering topknot, with individual strands coiled and intertwined; though the overall profile is somewhat flattened, the bound braids remain clearly discernible—tightly wound like hemp rope—demonstrating a mastery of sculptural control. Centered directly at the front of the topknot appears a small manifestation of Amitabha Buddha; this detail not only identifies the figure as Avalokiteśvara but also explicitly establishes his affiliation with the Lotus Family. Flanking the ears, fan-shaped crown ties spread outward horizontally, with their ribbons sweeping upward—a distinct decorative flourish that, far from disrupting the figure's overall solemnity, instead infuses its stability with a sense of upward-soaring vitality.

Turning to the face, this figure of Avalokiteśvara embodies nearly every captivating facial convention characteristic of the mature Pala sculptural style. A rectangular *urna* (tuft of hair) adorns the forehead, while the eyes are inlaid with silver and the lips with red copper—elements that create a remarkably subtle chromatic contrast against the brass-like hue of the facial skin. This interplay of materials and colors serves a purpose beyond mere opulence; it strikes a delicate balance between realism, divinity, and ornamentation. The high, arching eyebrows flow naturally into the bridge of the nose, which is tall and prominent; the lips are full yet taper slightly toward the center. The face draws upon realistic anthropological features characteristic of South Asia, yet undergoes an idealized refinement, resulting in a divine countenance that is simultaneously grounded in reality and transcendent of it. In other words, this face does not merely "depict Avalokiteśvara"; rather, it answers a profound question: How can a Bodhisattva retain the warmth of humanity while simultaneously radiating a splendor that transcends the human realm?

The treatment of the body serves as another hallmark of the mature Pala sculptural period. Avalokiteśvara possesses broad shoulders, a robust chest, and rounded arms; the entire torso stands tall and erect, yet remains free of any stiffness. The artisan did not rely on exaggerated ornamentation to obscure the underlying form; instead, through meticulous polishing and surface modeling, they directly sculpted the contours of the pectoral muscles, the subtle undulations of the abdomen, and the curves of the limbs—rendering the human anatomy with both clarity and naturalness. Most noteworthy here is that this naturalistic realism is not a passive replication of the physical body as it exists in reality,

but rather an idealized treatment imbued with a classical sensibility. The body appears at once robust and restrained—powerful yet devoid of coarseness—demonstrating the craftsman’s profound understanding of the concept of the "Bodhisattva’s Body": it must be more complete, more stable, and more capable of embodying the sacred than any physical form found in the real world.

Across the Bodhisattva’s chest rests a single-strand necklace inlaid with silver, beneath which hangs a jeweled *Yingluo* (decorative garland); the upper arms are adorned with diamond-shaped armbands. The drapery across the chest, the sacred cord, the waist sash, and the hem of the skirt are rendered through a combination of silver inlay, red copper inlay, and incised line engraving. The brilliance of this execution lies in the fact that, despite the abundance of ornamentation, not a single element appears superfluous. Each material serves a distinct visual function: silver lends clarity to the contours, red copper enlivens specific details, while the incised lines evoke the tactile softness of fabric clinging to the body. Thus, the ornamentation ceases to be mere embellishment superimposed upon the form; instead, it becomes an integral part of the figure’s rhythmic composition.

Guanyin holds a lotus in the left hand, while the right hand hangs gently before the knee, forming the *Varada Mudra* (Gesture of Granting Wishes). The significance of this combination is unequivocal: on one side stands the purity symbolized by the lotus; on the other, the promise of deliverance and salvation expressed by the *Mudra*. The Bodhisattva does not sit aloof in an inaccessible realm, but rather faces sentient beings with a posture of abiding in this world and actively bestowing compassionate regard. Flowing upward from both sides, floral branches blossom at the shoulders, simultaneously reinforcing Guanyin’s affiliation with the Lotus Pantheon and infusing the entire sculpture—otherwise grounded in stability—with the vibrant vitality of life in full expansion. Through the interplay of floral branches, flowing ribbons, hand gestures, and gaze, the figure collectively articulates a "compassion that flows outward."

The inverted-and-everted lotus pedestal upon which the figure rests is likewise worthy of repeated contemplation. The borders along the upper and lower edges feature a "continuous pearl" motif—rendered with plump, full beads arranged in rigorous precision. The lowest tier, in particular, displays an exaggerated richness; the beads appear almost as if they were individually set gemstones, creating a powerful visual rhythm. Lotus

The lotus petals, arranged in a full circle, are flat yet gracefully unfurled, staggered in a harmonious, rhythmic pattern. Between the petals and the lotus pedestal, three bands of red copper are inlaid, enriching the entire base with a heightened sense of depth through the interplay of contrasting metallic tones. On the reverse side of the pedestal, two lines of ancient Sanskrit—rendered in a script that is at once delicate and vigorous—further attest that this is no ordinary work; rather, it is a piece of exceptional caliber, imbued with a distinct religious identity and a specific context of devotional offering.

When these individual details are viewed once again within the context of the whole, one realizes that this figure of the World-Honored Avalokiteśvara stands as a quintessential exemplar of the mature Pala period not merely because it conforms to a checklist of stylistic traits, but because it masterfully resolves several of the most fundamental challenges inherent to Pala iconography: First, how to strike a balance between realism and the divine; second, how to maintain overall structural order amidst lavish ornamentation; and third, how to ensure that compassion remains not merely an abstract concept, but is instead tangibly manifested through form, facial expression, and posture.

It is precisely for this reason that the aura of this Avalokiteśvara figure is far from monolithic. Its robust, upright physique conveys a sense of steadfastness and valor; its high, arching brows, slightly upturned lips, and gently lowered chin imbue it with a profound, tranquil compassion; the towering, mountain-like topknot and the fluttering ribbons rising skyward lend an air of solemnity and majestic dignity to its otherwise serene countenance; while the softness of the floral branches and budding blossoms seems to transform boundless love into celestial nectar—gently and quietly sprinkling it upon the world. These myriad, seemingly disparate forces have been harmonized by the artisan into a unified whole; consequently, the final impression conveyed by this sculpture is not that of a singular emotion, but rather that of an extraordinary, tranquil power. This "tranquil power" is not to be confused with mere silence or stillness; rather, it signifies a presence that—without the need for exaggeration or ostentation—possesses the profound weight and resonance to penetrate the very depths of the human heart.

Thus, when we stand before this World-Honored Avalokiteśvara, what we behold is far more than merely an 11th-century bronze sculpture. What we truly witness here is how the final golden age of Indian Buddhism distilled its aesthetics, beliefs, craftsmanship, and ideal of the perfected human into a single sculptural form; we also observe how a master artisan, utilizing metal, gemstones, lines, and proportions, transformed "compassion" from a mere recited word into a tangible form that can be directly perceived by the eye.

Note:

An inscription in Sanskrit is engraved upon the lotus petals at the rear of the bronze statue's pedestal. According to the interpretation of Kashinath Tamot, this inscription is written in a variant of the ancient Kutila script—specifically, a regionalized form of the Nāgarī script prevalent in Northern India around the 11th century CE.1 The specific content reads as follows:

“देवव_गय_ झांव_लक नकष पू_यपाहवकस ।।”;

Romanized Transliteration: Devavaggarya Jhāṃmveṃlika nikaṣa pūṇyapālhalokasa ||;

Chinese Translation: "Puṇyapālhalokasa reverently bows in homage to (the Holy) Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva."

Regarding the personal name mentioned in the inscription—"Puṇyapālhalokasa"—his identity and dates remain currently undeterminable, though it may be surmised that he was a Buddhist monk hailing from India. Of particular note is the fact that the inscription, when referring to Avalokiteśvara Bodhisattva, does not employ the traditional Sanskrit appellation "Avalokiteśvara"; instead, it adopts the Tibetan appellation "Chenrezig" (སྤྱན་རས་གཟིགས), which was then phonetically transcribed into the Sanskrit form "Jhāṃmveṃlika" before being engraved.2 Evidently, this approach was adopted to fully accommodate the phonetic difficulties inherent in pronouncing the Sanskrit name of Avalokiteśvara within the Tibetan linguistic context, thereby demonstrating a strong tendency toward convergence with Tibetan traditions. Among the Pala-style sculptural inscriptions deciphered to date, this represents an extremely rare instance; a similar phenomenon is observed only in the Pala-style stone carvings discovered at Jyelakhang in Lhundrub County, Xizang.3 We may therefore conclude that this bronze statue was commissioned by an Indian patron specifically to fulfill the devotional needs of a Tibetan context.

Excerpted from an article by Ma Meimei

Notes:

1. This refers to the inscription transcription provided by Kashinath Tamot: *An Inscription of a Padmapāṇi Avalokiteśvara*, October 5, 2020. Personal communication. 2. *Ibid.* (see Note 1).

3. The inscription on the stone statue of Maitreya Bodhisattva at Jyelakhang was likewise engraved using the method of transcribing Sanskrit into the Tibetan script; according to the views of Shawokarsang, this stone statue of Maitreya Bodhisattva was likely sculpted by Tibetan artisans locally within Xizang. See Shawokarsang: "An Investigative Study of Pala-style Stone Sculptures and Inscriptions at Jyelakhang in Lhundrub County, Xizang." Chengdu: Sichuan University Press, *Journal of Tibetan Studies*, Issue 22 (Vol. 1, 2020), pp. 81–98, Plate 45.

excerpt.en: A compact Hevajra bronze of rhythmic silhouette and controlled force.

viewingNote.zh: 流动、梦幻，让人立刻想到佛教里常说的“空性”。这尊造像，从 第一眼就紧紧抓住了我的视线 。祂的力量带着一种向上迸发的劲 儿，扑面而来 。虽然初看会觉得很柔和，可仔细一感受，又能体会到 其中的攻击性 。这不是矛盾，而是一种刚柔并济的平衡，是一种形 式美本身所具备的张力。 如果从造像的形体入手去观察：高耸的发髻像火焰一般，发髻 上刻着十字金刚杵；头顶五叶概念化塑造的骷颅冠；一手高举金刚 杵，一手捧着颅碗，肩头倚着金刚杖；身上披挂着人头蔓，在虚空中 起舞 。这些特征都指向一个非常鲜明的题材——喜金刚 。祂又被称 为“饮血金刚”，是藏传佛教密宗修习的五大本尊之一，也是无上瑜伽 部的重要本尊。喜金刚的“喜”，指的是“大乐”；而“金刚”则象征 着坚不可摧的智慧与力量。合起来，就是“以不可摧毁的智慧所获得 的究竟大乐”。在萨迦派里，喜金刚几乎是核心本尊，很多修法都围 绕着《喜金刚续》展开；而在噶举、格鲁等派别中，虽然有不同的主 尊，比如胜乐金刚、密集金刚，但喜金刚同样受到修持和尊崇。 在密宗的语境中，舞蹈并不仅仅是身体的动作，而是一种“觉悟 之舞”。它意味着本尊的能量在宇宙与人心之间流转，也意味着智慧 在动态中被传递 。舞蹈的姿态不仅传达力量与活力，更在提醒修行 者：真正的智慧不是静止的，而是在运动、在变化 、在流转中显现出 来的——所谓“动中证空、动中生智”。而祂手中的颅碗 、身上的人头 蔓，则是极其直白的象征：生命本身是无常的，“我”的执念终究要 消解在虚空 。那些人头，并不是为了吓人，而是作为一种提醒——当 我们执着于自我，执着于欲望和身份时，痛苦就会随之而来；而当我 们敢于直面无常，才能真正理解生命的意义 。佛教艺术里的每一个 细节，都不是随意的装饰，而是一种语言。祂的眼睛嵌银，清澈而有穿透力，好像能一下子看穿众生内心 的欲望、破碎与遗憾 。红铜嵌饰的冠叶、嘴唇、项链 ，在视觉上起 到很好的点缀，华丽却毫不庸俗，也彰显了供养人对这尊造像的重 视 。祂的舞姿充满张力，身躯的线条像有弹性的弦，既柔软又充满 爆发力，让人有一种“梦幻”般的观感 。祂单脚站立于支点之上，似 乎随时会腾空而起，但力量却稳稳地从那一点向上生长，像植物破 土而出般蓬勃。这是一种生命向上的冲劲，也体现了匠人对构图美 学的把握 。 雕塑作为一种立体艺术，它的“语言”跟绘画完全不同。绘画 是平面的，是二维的关系；而雕塑是三维空间的，它依靠形体 、比 例、节奏、空间感与质感来构建审美的秩序。一件美的雕塑不只是 “修饰”的堆砌，而是“显现”。它把精神与意义转化为一种可见的形 式，让观看者不仅能看到形，还能感受到背后的精神。尤其在佛教 造像中，这种形式美往往是一种“觉悟之相”的表达，是一种精神永 恒性的呈现 。形式也并不只是表面的“好看”，而是一种深层的符 号——它让人即便不懂佛教的经义，也能在形体中感受到纯粹的“力 量之美”，或者“宁静之美”。 就像这尊喜金刚：祂的线条流动、姿态变幻，让人感觉到祂真 的在虚空中舞动。祂的力量不是藏在内部的，而是直白地具有冲击 性，是扑面而来的。那是一种精神融入形体之后，自然而然散发出 的美 。祂带给我们的感受是一种迷、一种幻，和祂的精神语言极度 契合。那是一种变幻的瞬间性——无常、空性 。而匠人抓住了这个 瞬间，让造像既不僵硬，又气韵充沛。所以，当我们凝视祂时，不仅 仅是在看一尊雕塑，而是在被提醒：一切都是流动的，都是无常的。 而正是在这种流动与幻象中，智慧与觉悟才真正显现 。
viewingNote.en: Fluid and dreamlike, this piece immediately brings to mind the concept of "emptiness" (Sunyata) so often discussed in Buddhism. From the very first glance, this sculptural image seized my attention completely. Its power radiates with an explosive, upward-surging energy that seems to rush right toward the viewer. Although it may appear gentle at first sight, a closer, more contemplative look reveals an underlying intensity—even a certain aggression. This is not a contradiction, but rather a balance of strength and softness—a tension inherent in the aesthetic form itself. If we examine the sculpture's physical attributes: a towering topknot rises like a flame, surmounted by a carved double-vajra; atop the head rests a crown of five stylized skulls; one hand is raised high holding a vajra, while the other cradles a skull-cup, with a ritual staff resting against the shoulder; draped across the body is a garland of human heads, as the deity dances amidst the void. All these characteristics point to a very specific subject: Hevajra. Also known as the "Blood-Drinking Vajra," Hevajra is one of the five principal deities practiced within the esoteric traditions of Tibetan Buddhism, and a key deity within the Highest Yoga Tantra class. In the name "Hevajra," the element "He" signifies "Great Bliss," while "Vajra" symbolizes indestructible wisdom and power. Taken together, the name denotes "the ultimate Great Bliss attained through indestructible wisdom." Within the Sakya school, Hevajra serves as virtually the central deity, with many ritual practices revolving around the *Hevajra Tantra*; in other schools such as the Kagyu and Gelug—which may focus on different principal deities such as Chakrasamvara or Guhyasamaja—Hevajra is nonetheless revered and actively practiced. In the context of esoteric Buddhism, dance is not merely a physical movement, but rather a "Dance of Enlightenment." It signifies the flow of the deity's energy between the cosmos and the human heart, and represents the transmission of wisdom through dynamic motion. The postures of the dance convey not only power and vitality but also serve as a reminder to the practitioner: true wisdom is not static; rather, it reveals itself through movement, change, and flux—encapsulated in the maxim: "Realizing emptiness through motion; generating wisdom through motion." The skull-cup held in His hand and the garland of human heads adorning His body serve as starkly direct symbols: life itself is impermanent, and the ego’s attachments are ultimately destined to dissolve into the void. These human heads are not intended to frighten, but rather to serve as a reminder: when we cling to the self—to our desires and identities—suffering inevitably follows; only when we dare to confront impermanence can we truly grasp the meaning of life. In Buddhist art, every single detail is not merely arbitrary ornamentation, but a form of language. His eyes, inlaid with silver, are clear and penetrating, appearing capable of instantly seeing through the desires, brokenness, and regrets hidden within the hearts of all sentient beings. The copper inlay adorning His crown leaves, lips, and necklace serves as a superb visual embellishment—luxurious yet never vulgar—and also underscores the profound reverence the patron held for this sculpture. His dance posture is charged with tension; the lines of His body resemble taut, elastic strings—simultaneously fluid and bursting with explosive energy—evoking a dreamlike visual sensation in the viewer. Standing on a single foot atop a pivot point, He appears poised to take flight at any moment; yet, power flows steadily upward from that single point of contact, surging with the vitality of a plant breaking through the soil. This embodies the upward thrust of life force and demonstrates the craftsman’s mastery of compositional aesthetics. As a three-dimensional art form, the "language" of sculpture differs entirely from that of painting. While painting is planar—existing within a two-dimensional relationship—sculpture inhabits three-dimensional space, relying on form, proportion, rhythm, spatial dynamics, and texture to construct its aesthetic order. A beautiful sculpture is not merely an accumulation of decorative embellishments, but an act of "manifestation." It transmutes spirit and meaning into a visible form, enabling the viewer to perceive not only the physical shape but also the underlying spiritual essence. Particularly in Buddhist iconography, this formal beauty often serves as an expression of the "visage of enlightenment"—a manifestation of spiritual eternity. Furthermore, form is not merely a matter of superficial "prettiness," but a profound symbolic language; it allows the viewer—even one unfamiliar with Buddhist scriptures—to perceive within the physical form a pure "beauty of power" or a "beauty of serenity." Take, for instance, this figure of Hevajra: His flowing lines and shifting posture create the palpable sensation that He is truly dancing within the void. His power is not something concealed deep within; rather, it possesses a direct, visceral impact—a force that surges forth to meet us. It is a beauty that radiates spontaneously, born of the moment when spirit becomes fully embodied in form. The sensation He evokes in us is one of enchantment and illusion—a feeling that resonates profoundly with His spiritual language. It captures the fleeting nature of transformation—the essence of impermanence and emptiness. The artisan has seized upon this very instant, endowing the sculpture with a vitality that is neither rigid nor static, but instead brimming with spiritual resonance. Thus, when we gaze upon Him, we are not merely observing a statue; we are being reminded that all things are in flux, that all things are impermanent. And it is precisely within this ceaseless flow and illusion that wisdom and enlightenment truly manifest.

excerpt.en: A Nalanda image of Padmapani defined by calm modeling and measured grace.

viewingNote.zh: 那烂陀寺，最早可以追溯到公元 5 世纪笈多王朝时期 。说它是寺庙，其实 也不完全准确，它更像是一所大学——而且是世界上最早的、体系化的高等学 府之一。那时候，这里已经有了图书馆、研究机构，还有很成熟的教学体系。 比我们熟知的牛津 、剑桥都早了很多个世纪 。 在寺院最辉煌的时期，有一万多名学生，几千位老师，不光学习佛学，还有 逻辑学、语法学、医学、天文学等等。而且它不是那种“灌输式”教学，反而 更接近我们今天大学里讲的“导师制”，强调深入学习，也注重个人修行。 当然，那烂陀寺的意义远远不只是知识传播。它是当时整个大乘佛教思想 的重要中心，也是世界各地僧侣、学者相互交流的地方。它让东西方的文化在 这里汇流，也让佛教的智慧开始更广泛地传播，影响了中国西藏 、东南亚、汉 传佛教等许多传统 。 我们熟悉的玄奘“西天取经”中那个“西天”，其实指的主要就是印度，而 那烂陀寺，就是他此行最重要的目的地之一。大约公元 627 年，他从长安出 发，经历了 17 年的旅程，最终抵达那里 。在那烂陀寺，他深入学习中观 、瑜伽 行、因明等大乘佛学的核心理论，还在《大唐西域记》中详细记载了寺院的建 筑 、学习氛围和教学体系。他认为，这不仅是佛教的中心，更是当时整个印度 文化思想的代表 。 但历史总有令人沉默的部分。到了公元 1193 年，那烂陀寺遭遇毁灭性的打 击，被异教统治者焚毁 。藏书燃烧了好几个月，僧侣被杀 。寺院不复存在，也 象征着一个文明中心的终结。后来，佛教文化的重心也就逐渐向中国西藏 、日 本等地转移。 文化的断裂，往往也意味着艺术品的毁灭 。于是随着寺院的终结，其造像 也几乎都被埋进了土里，只剩下了少量塔藏和当时被带离开印度的造像，能有 幸得以保留下来 。所以现在我们能在各大公、私馆藏和古董市场中见到的那烂 陀寺造像是及少的。而那烂坨寺由于位于恒河流域 ，所以由其以寺院名称命名 的造像艺术在受到了笈多、秣菟罗、犍陀罗等多种风格的影响后，逐渐发展出了 具有标志性的那烂坨风格。 我们眼前的这尊 9-10 世纪典型的那烂陀寺造像，第一眼给我的感觉是完 整。因为我们能见到的、保存下来了的那烂陀寺造像头顶上的滑盖大多都是丢 失的。其次，是祂在历史中的重要性，已知存留下来的造像数量，放在整个佛 教艺术中，是非常稀少和珍贵的。再就是，可能原来可能窖藏后继续传世的温 润皮壳，有一种时间流转的痕迹 。 观音面容柔和，笑容和煦 。形体并不魁伟，反而显得轻盈而从容，整体给 人一种非常温柔的气息，感觉只要一见到，就会被祂的开心所感染 。观音头戴 宝冠，宝冠顶端为庄严的化佛，再上方是一顶清凉华盖，象征庄严。右手结与 愿印，左手持一朵带着莲茎的莲花，脚踏一朵仿佛从莲座中自然长出的莲 花，姿态挺拔、自在，背后则是一轮圆形背光 。尽管造像的元素很多，但从背 光上就能看出，纹饰都是一些便简单的几何、圆弧、线条为主，所以使得整尊 造像在繁复中并不显笨重，而是呈现出一种轻盈和典雅 。 莲座上是宽厚大片的莲瓣，莲瓣片片相连，这种特征为典型的那烂陀寺风 格，像是一朵绽放的莲花 。莲座前方的莲瓣上刻有梵文题记，这是在那烂陀风 格的造像中少见的一种表现方式 。同样，背光的后面也围绕着形状刻了一圈古 梵文文字，这种书写方式，也同样是那烂陀造像风格惯用的一种表达方式 。 由于造像原来可能是属于窖藏的一种储存环境，皮克会略显斑驳，但经过 后世手心的温度接触，透出了其原本的铜质，会有一种时间感 。每次看到这些 早期的造像，当握在手中时，总会感觉到一种时间的厚重，心总能慢慢沉下 来，像是在提醒：别着急，慢慢来 。 也许是我们现在生活节奏太快了，没时间停下来，所以对时间的感知并不 敏锐；又或者，是那个时代真的离我们太遥远了，以至于我们很容易忽略掉这 些雕塑，见证着我们人类走过了一段非常重要的路。我们今天拥有的一切，不 论是知识、艺术 ，还是信仰、精神，其实都不是凭空出现的。它们是很多很多 代人走过来的结果，是我们和过去之间的一条线 。这些看似沉默的造像，恰恰 告诉我们：我们从哪里来，往哪里去，也许并不需要语言来回答，只需要我们 愿意静下来，去看、去听、去感受 ...
viewingNote.en: The origins of Nalanda Monastery can be traced back as far as the 5th century CE, during the Gupta Dynasty. To label it merely a "monastery" would not be entirely accurate; it functioned more as a university—and indeed, one of the world's earliest and most systematically organized institutions of higher learning. Even in that era, it boasted a library, research institutes, and a highly sophisticated pedagogical system—predating the likes of Oxford and Cambridge, institutions familiar to us today, by many centuries. During its zenith, the monastery was home to over ten thousand students and thousands of teachers, who engaged in the study of not only Buddhist philosophy but also logic, grammar, medicine, astronomy, and various other disciplines. Moreover, its educational approach was not one of rote "indoctrination"; rather, it bore a closer resemblance to the "mentorship model" prevalent in modern universities, emphasizing in-depth study while simultaneously prioritizing individual spiritual cultivation. Of course, the significance of Nalanda Monastery extended far beyond the mere dissemination of knowledge. It served as a pivotal hub for Mahayana Buddhist thought throughout the region at the time, as well as a nexus where monks and scholars from across the globe converged to exchange ideas. It acted as a confluence where Eastern and Western cultures intermingled, facilitating the broader propagation of Buddhist wisdom and influencing numerous traditions, including those of Tibet, Southeast Asia, and Chinese Buddhism. The "Western Heaven" (or "Western Paradise")—the fabled destination of Xuanzang's renowned "Journey to the West"—referred primarily to India, and Nalanda Monastery stood as one of the most critical destinations of his entire pilgrimage. Setting out from Chang'an around 627 CE, he embarked on a seventeen-year odyssey before finally reaching its gates. At Nalanda, he immersed himself in the core tenets of Mahayana Buddhism—including the Madhyamaka (Middle Way), Yogacara (Consciousness-Only), and Hetuvidya (Buddhist Logic) schools—and meticulously documented the monastery's architecture, academic atmosphere, and educational framework in his *Records of the Western Regions* (Da Tang Xi Yu Ji). He regarded it not merely as the epicenter of Buddhism, but as the very embodiment of Indian culture and intellectual thought of that era. Yet, history inevitably holds its somber chapters. In 1193 CE, Nalanda Monastery suffered a catastrophic blow when it was razed by invading rulers. Its vast library burned for months on end, and its monks were massacred. The monastery ceased to exist, marking—symbolically as well as literally—the demise of a great center of civilization. Subsequently, the gravitational center of Buddhist culture gradually shifted toward regions such as Tibet, China, and Japan. The rupture of a cultural lineage, all too often, entails the destruction of its artistic heritage. Consequently, with the demise of the monastery, nearly all of its sculptural works were buried beneath the earth; only a small number of relics preserved within stupas—along with those few pieces carried away from India at the time—were fortunate enough to survive. As a result, the Nalanda sculptures visible today in major public and private collections, as well as in the antiquities market, are exceedingly rare. Situated within the Ganges River basin, the Nalanda monastery gave its name to a distinct sculptural tradition; having absorbed influences from various artistic schools—including the Gupta, Mathura, and Gandhara styles—this tradition gradually evolved into the iconic "Nalanda style." The 9th–10th century Nalanda sculpture before us strikes me, at first glance, with its remarkable completeness. This is significant because, among the surviving Nalanda sculptures visible today, the decorative canopy or finial surmounting the head is, in most cases, missing. Secondly, the piece holds immense historical importance; given the extremely limited number of known surviving examples, it stands as a rare and precious treasure within the broader context of Buddhist art. Furthermore, the sculpture bears a lustrous patina—likely the result of having been buried in a cache before passing down through the ages—that carries the unmistakable traces of the passage of time. The countenance of Guanyin is gentle, and the smile is warm and benevolent. The figure is not massive in stature; rather, it appears light and poised, exuding an overall aura of profound tenderness—one feels that, upon merely beholding it, one is instantly uplifted by its radiant joy. Guanyin wears a jeweled crown, surmounted by a solemn miniature Buddha figure; above this rests a graceful canopy, symbolizing divine majesty. The right hand forms the *Varada Mudra* (Gesture of Granting Wishes), while the left holds a lotus blossom complete with its stem; the feet rest upon a lotus bloom that appears to have sprouted organically from the lotus pedestal itself. The posture is upright yet relaxed and natural, framed by a circular *mandorla* (halo) at the back. Although the sculpture incorporates numerous decorative elements, the design of the *mandorla* reveals that the ornamentation relies primarily on simple geometric shapes, arcs, and linear motifs; this aesthetic choice ensures that, despite its intricate detailing, the sculpture never appears heavy or cumbersome, but instead conveys an impression of lightness and elegance. The lotus pedestal features broad, substantial petals—each seamlessly connected to the next—a characteristic hallmark of the classic Nalanda style, evoking the image of a fully blossomed lotus flower. Inscribed upon the lotus petals situated at the front of the pedestal are Sanskrit characters—a mode of artistic expression rarely encountered within the Nalanda sculptural tradition. Similarly, the reverse side of the *mandorla* (halo) features a band of ancient Sanskrit script encircling its contours—a stylistic convention frequently employed in Nalanda-period statuary. Given that this sculpture likely originated from a buried hoard, its patina appears somewhat mottled; yet, through the warmth of countless hands holding it over the centuries, the underlying bronze metal has begun to gleam through, imbuing the piece with a profound sense of the passage of time. Whenever I encounter these early sculptures—especially when holding one in my hands—I am invariably struck by the sheer weight of history they embody. My mind begins to settle, slowly finding its calm, as if the object itself were whispering a gentle reminder: "Do not rush; take your time." Perhaps the pace of modern life has become too frenetic, leaving us with no time to pause and reflect, and thus dulling our sensitivity to the passage of time. Or perhaps that bygone era simply lies too far in the distant past—so remote, in fact, that we easily overlook these sculptures, failing to recognize them as silent witnesses to a pivotal chapter in the journey of humanity. Everything we possess today—whether it be knowledge, art, faith, or spirit—did not, in truth, spring into existence out of thin air. These treasures are the cumulative legacy of countless generations that have walked this path before us; they serve as a vital thread connecting us to our past. These seemingly silent sculptures speak to us most eloquently, offering an answer to the fundamental questions of where we come from and where we are headed—an answer that requires no spoken words, but merely our willingness to quiet our minds, to look, to listen, and to feel...
