Its lustrous patina bears silent witness to the centuries of devout veneration it has received; time itself has proven to be the most astonishing of artisans, imparting—through its subtle, perfectly balanced wear—a unique aesthetic beauty that is truly delightful. A faint smile plays upon the corners of his lips—radiant as if bathed in sunlight, gentle and warm—causing the viewer's heart to spontaneously fill with joy. This sculpture of Maitreya exemplifies the quintessential aesthetic sensibilities of the Swat region. He wears a jeweled crown adorned with pointed leaf motifs, his hair is gathered into a high topknot, and braids cascade naturally down over his shoulders. His eyebrows are gracefully arched, his face is oval-shaped, and his chin is full and rounded; his eyes—originally inlaid with silver—radiate an expression that is at once gentle and steadfast. Beneath his high, prominent nose lie lips inlaid with red copper, bearing that faint smile—radiant as if bathed in sunlight, gentle and warm—that causes the viewer's heart to spontaneously fill with joy. The artisan has masterfully blended naturalistic realism with idealistic representation, presenting us with the image of an ascetic cultivating the path within the mundane world—devoid of any imposing austerity, instead appearing utterly at ease and approachable. When viewed from various angles, one experiences a momentary sense of reverie, as if gazing upon a man from ancient Swat—a countenance that seems to silently articulate the boundless truths of the Dharma. Beyond the naturalistic rendering of his facial features, his right hand—raised in the *Abhaya Mudra* (the gesture of fearlessness)—exhibits a distinctive feature: webbing (*jālānaddha*) between the fingers. This characteristic stylistic trait perpetuates the aesthetic legacy of the Gupta Empire and serves as iconographic evidence confirming the sculpture's provenance within the early period of Swat Buddhist art. The broad, substantial palms emphasize the characteristics of the *mudra* (hand gesture)—one that instills peace and fearlessness in all sentient beings. The *kalasha* (ritual vase) held in the left hand serves here as an iconographic identifier of Maitreya Bodhisattva. The lines of the torso are rendered with simplicity and an air of archaic elegance. The long skirt adorning the lower body is depicted as having a heavy texture; through the technique of incised line carving, the artisan has vividly captured the folds and realistic drape of the garment. The lotus pedestal beneath—characterized by a deeply constricted waist and smooth, expansive petals—exhibits distinct regional features of the Swat Valley. It radiates a sense of grandeur and openness, while simultaneously infusing the sculpture with boundless vitality. Among surviving examples of Swat Valley statuary, the subject of Maitreya Bodhisattva is relatively rare. This particular figure—a 7th to 8th-century Swat depiction of Maitreya—bears the patina of over a millennium of time; through this passage of ages, the sculpture has acquired a warm, amber-like luster that seems to emanate from within. As time has flowed by, this figure of Maitreya has come to exude an aura of profound tranquility, inviting the viewer to become immersed in a spiritual state of peace and joy.

excerpt.en: A standing Vajrapani that joins early Xizang power with Nepalese finesse.

viewingNote.zh: 在那悠久、遥远的八至九世纪，青藏高原之巅，吐蕃王朝如一颗璀璨的星 辰，照亮了古代世界的东方。吐蕃王朝，一个由松赞干布一手缔造的强大政权。 在全盛时期，其疆域辽阔，不仅囊括了今天中国的西藏地区，还延伸至青海、四 川、云南的部分地区，甚至一度控制了河西走廊。这样的地理优势使得吐蕃成为 了丝绸之路南线的重要枢纽。在这样的背景下，吐蕃不仅促进了商品的互通有 无，更在思想、宗教、艺术等领域引发了深刻的碰撞与融合，吸收了中原、南 亚、中亚等地的文化元素。在这样强大的背景下，吐蕃王朝展现出了前所未有的 文化自信和创造力。在将本土文化继续传承和发展的基础上，勇于吸收外来文化 的精华，将其融入自己的文化体系之中，这种开放包容的文化态度，使得吐蕃的 文化艺术呈现出多元并蓄的特点。 在吐蕃王朝的艺术作品中，既可以看到本土文化的原始与粗犷，又能感受到 外来文化的细腻与精致。这种交融并非简单的拼凑与堆砌，而是经过匠人们的精 心设计与巧妙构思，将两种截然不同的美学风格完美地融合在一起。正是在这样 的历史背景中，一尊金刚手立像应运而生。金刚手立像的诞生，不仅是吐蕃与尼 泊尔文化交流的见证，它更展示了不同文明之间和谐共生的可能性，证明了在尊 重差异、包容多元的基础上，人类能够创造出更加丰富多彩的文化成果。 金刚手菩萨，因手执金刚杵，常侍卫于佛前，故称之为金刚手菩萨，他与观 音菩萨、文殊菩萨合称“三怙主”，分别代表“慈悲、智慧、力量”三种特质。在 此尊金刚手菩萨的身上可以看见非常明显的尼泊尔风格，与同时期尼泊尔的理查 维美学风格有诸多相似之处。尽管风格如出一辙，但在相同中也带有些不同之 处。在七世纪中期，松赞干布先后迎娶了尼泊尔的尺尊公主和唐王朝的文成公主 后，这两位公主不约而同的都笃信佛教，他们随身携带了佛像、法物以及许多经 典，并分别邀请了两地的匠人建造了大昭寺和小昭寺，用以供奉她们带来的佛 像，这标志着佛教正式传入了西藏，并产生了深远的影响，在现今大昭寺的门楣 上我们依旧可以看到尼泊尔匠人留下的作品（图一）。金刚手菩萨头戴三叶冠，帽冠两侧为圆形花朵的冠饰，发髻线的发丝整齐有 序、根根分明，宽长的耳朵配带有花朵型耳饰。两屡富有弹性的头发分别垂于两 肩，头发上的蓝彩和头部以及身上的泥金，表明此尊金刚手菩萨原本一直被供 奉。线刻的弯眉高挑，眉中的白毫同样以线刻的方式体现；鼻梁高挺，嘴角带 笑，双目低垂、视线向下，眼神深邃而有力，透露出一种不可言喻的威严与慈悲 并存的复杂情感。脸部整体圆润丰盈，弱化骨骼感的塑造，为唐代中原造像惯用 的表现特征，宽长的耳型与脸庞中透着一丝唐代造像韵味。菩萨佩戴项链、臂 钏、手环，一手持金刚杵（金刚杵由于某些原因断裂），一手微抬结无畏印。腰部 佩戴连珠纹样式的腰带，腰间肌肉紧实、微凸，下身着印度传统裙裤，双腿稳站 于圆形的仰覆式莲台之上。莲瓣宽大舒展，刻有理查维造像上典型的川字纹。 此件金刚手菩萨，当第一眼看过去时，也许并不会立刻抓住我们眼球，但是 越看，视线就越无法挪开，菩萨五官的塑造，似有一种润物细无声的情绪输出。 肩部塑造宽大，腰部及腿部修长，比例精确、不局限细节的表现手法，有种大开 大合、不拘小节、明朗开阔的视觉感，加上微微前倾的体态，使得整件作品尺寸 虽不大，但却有大佛的气势，气场浑厚、充满力量感，当面对他时，内心不由自主 地涌现一股强烈的敬畏。这种风格的塑造主要凸显出菩萨内在精神的传达，而非 图一 布达拉宫 8世纪 雕刻作品 图四 尼泊尔9世纪 金刚手与协侍菩萨 图五 布达拉宫收藏 图二 敦煌绢画 8-9世纪 金刚手菩萨 图三 尼泊尔 5世纪 摩耶夫人之佛陀降生 外在形式的雕琢，使得作品具有一种超越时代的艺术美感。板正站立的身姿，并 不像同时期尼泊尔造像般曲线动态明显，反而和敦煌出土的绢画有着同样的风格 特征（图二）。从雕塑的公约数而言，这种板正、直挺的表现方式，与王室需要的 硬朗、正派气质更为符合，如埃及的正面率、如宗教艺术品中重要人物的结构排 列和尺寸大小，都代表着不同的地位和重要性。从匠人的角度推测，此尊金刚手 菩萨在带有明显唐代开脸韵味，又具有明显尼泊尔图像风格的同时，但又区别于 尼泊尔早期或同时期造像那般明显的三折姿体态（图三、图四），结合上这些图像 学及经验判断的同时，再结合上造像皮壳上千年来的供养状态推测，此尊金刚手 菩萨应该为尼泊尔匠人或者藏地匠人，在受到多元文化影响后于西藏制作，并于 西藏重要寺庙传世（图五）。此件金刚手立像不论从年代、地域的稀缺性，还是整 体美感、品相的完整度而言，都属于极为难得的存在。 吐蕃作为千年前的国际化大都城，其历史地位、文化贡献和国际影响力都是不 可忽视的。这件金刚手立像记录着那个伟大王朝的一切面貌，如今我们还能有幸 通过这件作品一窥当年那个国际化王朝的影响力和辉煌，何其有幸、何其不易。
viewingNote.en: In the distant and storied era of the 8th and 9th centuries, atop the Qinghai-Tibet Plateau, the Tubo Dynasty shone like a brilliant star, illuminating the eastern reaches of the ancient world. The Tubo Dynasty was a formidable polity single-handedly established by Songtsen Gampo. At its zenith, its vast territory encompassed not only the modern-day Tibet Autonomous Region of China but also extended into parts of Qinghai, Sichuan, and Yunnan, and even—for a time—controlled the Hexi Corridor. This strategic geographical advantage positioned Tubo as a vital hub along the southern route of the Silk Road. Against this backdrop, Tubo not only facilitated the exchange of goods but also sparked profound encounters and fusions across the realms of thought, religion, and art, assimilating cultural elements from the Central Plains, South Asia, and Central Asia. Bolstered by this powerful foundation, the Tubo Dynasty demonstrated an unprecedented level of cultural confidence and creativity. While steadfastly preserving and developing its indigenous traditions, it boldly embraced the finest elements of foreign cultures, seamlessly integrating them into its own cultural framework; this open and inclusive cultural ethos endowed Tubo’s arts and culture with a distinctive character—one that was both diverse and eclectic. Within the artworks of the Tubo Dynasty, one can discern both the primal vigor and ruggedness of indigenous culture and the subtlety and refinement of foreign traditions. This fusion was no mere patchwork or haphazard assemblage; rather, through the meticulous design and ingenious conceptualization of master craftsmen, two distinct aesthetic styles were blended together in perfect harmony. It was precisely within this historical context that a standing statue of Vajrapani came into being. The creation of this standing Vajrapani statue serves not only as a testament to the cultural exchange between Tubo and Nepal but also as a demonstration of the potential for harmonious coexistence among different civilizations—proving that, by respecting differences and embracing diversity, humanity can create cultural achievements of even greater richness and vibrancy. Vajrapani Bodhisattva—so named for the *vajra* (thunderbolt scepter) he holds in his hand while standing guard before the Buddha—is revered alongside Avalokiteśvara (Guanyin) and Mañjuśrī as one of the "Three Lords of the World" (or "Three Protectors"), representing the three distinct qualities of Compassion, Wisdom, and Power. This figure of Vajrapani Bodhisattva exhibits a distinctly Nepalese style, bearing numerous similarities to the aesthetic conventions of the Licchavi period in Nepal. Although the stylistic parallels are striking, closer examination reveals subtle differences amidst the similarities. In the mid-7th century, after King Songtsen Gampo successively married Princess Bhrikuti of Nepal and Princess Wencheng of the Tang Dynasty, both princesses—being devout Buddhists—brought with them Buddhist images, ritual implements, and numerous scriptures. They subsequently commissioned artisans from their respective homelands to construct the Jokhang and Ramoche Temples to enshrine the images they had introduced; this event marked the formal introduction of Buddhism into Tibet and exerted a profound and lasting influence on the region. Indeed, works left behind by Nepalese artisans can still be observed today on the lintels of the Jokhang Temple (Figure 1). Vajrapani Bodhisattva wears a tripartite crown adorned with circular floral motifs on either side. The strands of hair along the hairline are arranged with meticulous precision, appearing distinct and orderly, while the long, broad earlobes are embellished with floral-shaped earrings. Two curling locks of hair cascade gracefully over the shoulders; traces of blue pigment in the hair, combined with the gilding on the head and body, indicate that this figure of Vajrapani Bodhisattva was historically an object of continuous veneration. The arched eyebrows are delineated with incised lines—a technique also used to depict the *urna* (the tuft of hair between the brows). The nose bridge is high and prominent, and a faint smile plays upon the lips. With eyes cast downward in a deep, penetrating gaze, the figure radiates a complex emotional aura—an ineffable blend of majesty and compassion. The face is rendered with a full, rounded plumpness that de-emphasizes skeletal contours—a stylistic characteristic frequently employed in sculptural traditions of the Tang Dynasty Central Plains. Furthermore, the broad, elongated earlobes and the overall facial physiognomy subtly evoke the aesthetic sensibilities of Tang-era Buddhist sculpture. The Bodhisattva is adorned with a necklace, armbands, and bracelets; one hand originally held a *vajra* (which has since broken off for reasons unknown), while the other is slightly raised to form the *Abhaya Mudra* (the gesture of fearlessness). A belt featuring a "continuous pearl" motif encircles the waist, where the musculature is rendered as firm and slightly bulging; the lower body is clad in traditional Indian-style drapery, and the figure stands firmly upon a circular lotus pedestal featuring both upward- and downward-facing petals. The lotus petals are broad and expansive, incised with the characteristic "Chuan-character" pattern typical of sculptures from the Licchavi period. At first glance, this figure of Vajrapani Bodhisattva may not immediately seize our attention; yet, the longer one gazes upon it, the more impossible it becomes to look away. The modeling of the Bodhisattva’s facial features conveys an emotional resonance that is subtle yet deeply penetrating—like a gentle rain that quietly nourishes the earth. The shoulders are sculpted broadly, while the waist and legs are slender; the proportions are precise, yet the artistic approach transcends mere meticulous detail. The piece exudes a visual sense of grandeur and openness—bold in its overall composition, unconstrained by trivialities, and radiantly expansive. This aesthetic, combined with a posture that leans ever so slightly forward, endows the work—despite its modest physical dimensions—with the imposing presence of a monumental Buddha. It possesses a profound aura and is charged with a palpable sense of power; when standing before it, one is involuntarily overcome by a profound sense of awe. This sculptural style primarily emphasizes the conveyance of the Bodhisattva’s inner spiritual essence, rather than—as illustrated in Fig. 1 (Potala Palace, 8th-century sculpture), Fig. 4 (Nepal, 9th-century Vajrapani and Attendant Bodhisattvas), Fig. 5 (Potala Palace Collection), Fig. 2 (Dunhuang silk painting, 8th–9th century, Vajrapani Bodhisattva), and Fig. 3 (Nepal, 5th century, Maya’s Conception of the Buddha)—a mere preoccupation with external formal ornamentation. Consequently, the work possesses an artistic beauty that transcends the boundaries of its specific era. Its upright, rigid stance—unlike the distinctly curvilinear and dynamic poses characteristic of Nepalese sculptures from the same period—instead shares stylistic affinities with the silk paintings unearthed at Dunhuang (Fig. 2). In terms of sculptural conventions, this upright, rigid mode of representation aligns more closely with the robust and dignified aesthetic sensibilities required by royalty. Much like the "frontalism" of ancient Egyptian art—or the structural arrangement and relative scale of key figures in religious iconography—such stylistic choices serve to signify distinct levels of status and importance. From the perspective of craftsmanship, this figure of Vajrapani Bodhisattva exhibits the distinct facial aesthetics characteristic of the Tang Dynasty, while simultaneously displaying the unmistakable iconographic style of Nepal. Yet, it diverges from early or contemporaneous Nepalese sculptures by lacking their characteristic *tribhanga* (triple-bend) posture (Figs. 3 & 4). Synthesizing these iconographic observations and expert judgments—and further taking into account the patina and surface condition resulting from a millennium of ritual veneration—it is surmised that this Vajrapani figure was crafted in Tibet by either Nepalese or Tibetan artisans working under the influence of a multicultural milieu, and that it has been preserved as a treasured heirloom within a major Tibetan monastery (Fig. 5). In terms of its rarity—both chronologically and geographically—as well as its overall aesthetic appeal and pristine condition, this standing figure of Vajrapani represents an exceptionally rare and remarkable artifact. As a cosmopolitan metropolis of a millennium ago, the Tubo Kingdom holds a historical stature, cultural significance, and international influence that simply cannot be overlooked. This standing figure of Vajrapani serves as a tangible record of every facet of that magnificent dynasty; that we are privileged today to catch a glimpse of that cosmopolitan empire's influence and glory through this very work is a stroke of immense fortune—and a privilege not easily attained.

label.en: Acquired from Kapoor Galleries, New York, 4 October 1984

label.zh: 詹姆斯和玛丽琳·阿尔斯多夫收藏，美国芝加哥。
label.en: The James and Marilyn Alsdorf Collection, Chicago

excerpt.en: A compact image of Jambhala charged with dense presence and protective energy.

viewingNote.zh: 第一次看到这尊黄财神造像时，我是被吸引住的。会有种直觉——这不仅 仅是一尊财神像，更像一位威严的将军，一位充满力量的护法，甚至还是一位 充满福相与富贵气息的神祇 。祂的姿态挺拔，饱满而端正，完全符合我们心 中对“财神”二字的想象和期待。因为在藏文化中黄财神，从来都不只是象征 财富的神祇那么简单，祂还是一位发了大愿、具足威德、象征护佑的护法神。 从艺术风格来看，这尊造像是一件 12-13 世纪西藏受到帕拉艺术风格影 响的一件作品 。在业内，往往一件作品如果出现了风格的融合，而且做得很出 色，人们第一反应通常是：“这可能是外来匠人做的。”因为传统上，大家会觉 得西藏本土匠人在那时候对于有文化融入的作品是不太容易处理好的。但由 于历史很难回望，很多事都无法过于绝对。所以，往往我更提倡不论是外来工 匠还是本土工匠，我们应该更多的注重作品本身的最终呈现 。 像这件作品，我觉得匠人不只是简单的“风格借用或者风格融合”，我更 愿意说，这是某位匠人通过深刻的理解 、消化之后，创造出来的属于匠人他自 己的艺术语言。这种语言既熟悉又新鲜，有继承 ，有变化，带着独属于他自己 的思考。 于时代背景而言，这件黄财神诞生在后弘期初期阿底峡入藏弘法之后，那 段文化交流频繁、艺术风格多元交织的时期 。当时帕拉艺术通过匠人、典 籍、法脉一路传入了青藏高原。随着 12 世纪中后期帕拉王朝的衰亡，西藏逐 渐成为大乘佛教、尤其是密宗的核心传承地 ，也因此迎来了一波新的文化融合 以及工艺流动。这尊黄财神，便是在这样的时代背景中诞生的。这件作品既 有帕拉艺术中结构严谨、线条流畅的优点，同时也将西藏本土的粗犷、阳刚和 那种山一般的力量感融入了其中，呈现出了一种“体积之美”。 财神发髻高高束起，头戴三叶冠，佩戴圆形耳铛，耳后后装饰有帕拉风格 常见的扇形冠结。脸型略方中带圆，双眼怒睁，但由于脸部五官的动态并没有 那么大，只是微微的，既给人一种护法的正气之感，又不会产生那种令人不安 的恐惧，反而让人感受到被保护的安心。 财神佩戴项链、璎珞、花环，下身是刻有细腻花纹的裙裤 。祂一手拿着 吐宝鼠，一手持摩尼宝珠 ，一脚踩着象征财富的圆形宝瓶 ，悠然地坐在莲台之 上 。整个坐姿既稳重又挺拔 。不论从正面或侧面看，形体饱满、宽厚 、结 实，且头、胸、手臂、腿部 、腰部等肢体都能看到明确区分与联系，转折自 然，形面之间起伏得当，不突兀 、不混乱，没有松散或空洞的感觉 。在整个坐 姿中，肚子的部分也很突出，圆圆的形状像球体一样饱满，轻轻地落在大腿 上，那种“福气满满”的视觉印象，是非常典型的黄财神形象特征，同样也使 得作品，给人“座得住、压得稳”的感觉 。这种由不动如山的气质所带给我们 的体积之美的构成，是经过匠人的反复观察和构造而来，才会使得作品既符 合这个题材想说的话，又具有审美的节奏与秩序。 同样，莲台也值得一提 。莲台是帕拉风格中常见的大型宽叶莲瓣设计，莲 瓣排列整齐、大气 、节奏明快 。上下缀满了连珠纹，一圈大、一圈小，层层递 进，整个莲台像一个层层递进的梯形结构，于视觉和结构上都增强了稳定感和 庄严感 。 王朝闻老师曾强调，“雕塑的体积感和量感，是一种体现雕塑实体性与审 美逻辑的内在因素，而非尺寸上的表面张扬。” 而这件财神造像就非常的符合 这句话，虽然尺寸不大，但在视觉心理上，且并不亚于一块大石头般的厚 重、沉稳 。匠人对实体存在的理解 、对形体结构的把握、对空间节奏的感 知，以及对形式与精神统一的追求达成了统一，很是难得。
viewingNote.en: The first time I laid eyes on this sculpture of the Yellow Jambhala, I was captivated. I felt an immediate intuition: this was not merely a statue of a god of wealth, but rather resembled a majestic general—a powerful Dharma Protector, and indeed, a deity radiating an aura of auspiciousness and opulence. His posture is upright, robust, and dignified—perfectly embodying the mental image and expectations we hold for the very concept of a "God of Wealth." For in Tibetan culture, the Yellow Jambhala has never been merely a deity symbolizing material wealth; He is also a Dharma Protector who has vowed to fulfill a Great Pledge, possessing immense power and virtue, and serving as a symbol of divine guardianship. From an artistic perspective, this sculpture is a 12th-to-13th-century Tibetan work that bears the distinct influence of the Pala artistic style. Within the industry, whenever a piece exhibits a fusion of styles—especially when executed with such exceptional skill—the initial reaction is often: "This was likely created by foreign artisans." This is because, traditionally, it was believed that indigenous Tibetan artisans of that era would have found it difficult to successfully execute works involving such cultural synthesis. However, given the difficulty of looking back through the mists of history, few things can be stated with absolute certainty. Consequently, I tend to advocate that—regardless of whether the creator was a foreign or indigenous artisan—our primary focus should remain on the final artistic presentation of the work itself. In the case of this particular piece, I feel the artisan did not merely engage in a simple "borrowing" or "fusion" of styles; rather, I would argue that, through profound understanding and assimilation, the artisan forged an artistic language entirely his own. This language feels simultaneously familiar and fresh—rooted in tradition yet marked by innovation, and imbued with a unique depth of personal reflection. In terms of historical context, this Yellow Jambhala was created during the early stages of the "Later Propagation" (Phyi-dar) period—specifically following the arrival of Atisha in Tibet to propagate the Dharma—an era characterized by vibrant cultural exchange and the intricate interweaving of diverse artistic styles. During this time, Pala art flowed into the Tibetan Plateau through various channels: the migration of artisans, the translation of canonical texts, and the transmission of spiritual lineages. With the decline of the Pala Dynasty in the mid-to-late 12th century, Tibet gradually emerged as the central hub for the transmission of Mahayana Buddhism—and particularly Vajrayana Buddhism—thereby ushering in a new wave of cultural synthesis and the cross-pollination of craftsmanship. It is against this rich historical backdrop that this magnificent sculpture of the Yellow Jambhala came into being. This piece embodies the virtues of Pala art—characterized by rigorous structure and fluid lines—while simultaneously integrating the ruggedness, virility, and mountain-like sense of power indigenous to Tibet, thereby manifesting a distinct "beauty of volume." The God of Wealth’s hair is gathered into a high topknot, surmounted by a three-lobed crown; he wears circular ear ornaments, and behind his ears are decorative, fan-shaped ribbons—a motif frequently seen in Pala-style iconography. His face is slightly square yet rounded in contour, with eyes wide open in a fierce gaze; however, the dynamic intensity of his facial features is subtle—merely a slight tension—which conveys the righteous authority of a Dharma Protector without evoking unsettling terror. Instead, it instills a sense of security and the assurance of being safeguarded. The deity is adorned with necklaces, elaborate festoons, and floral garlands, while his lower body is clad in trousers intricately carved with delicate floral patterns. In one hand, he holds a jewel-spitting mongoose; in the other, a wish-fulfilling gem (*Mani*). With one foot resting upon a round treasure vase—a symbol of wealth—he sits serenely upon a lotus pedestal. His overall seated posture is at once poised and upright. Viewed from either the front or the side, his physique appears full, broad, and robust; the various anatomical components—head, chest, arms, legs, and waist—are clearly delineated yet seamlessly interconnected. The transitions between forms are natural, and the interplay of surfaces exhibits a harmonious undulation—neither abrupt nor chaotic—devoid of any sense of looseness or hollowness. A particularly prominent feature of this seated figure is the belly; its rounded form is as full and spherical as a globe, resting gently upon his thighs. This visual impression of "overflowing blessings" is a quintessential characteristic of the Yellow God of Wealth (*Jambhala*) iconography, and it further endows the sculpture with a palpable sense of stability—as if it is "firmly rooted and immovably grounded." This "beauty of volume"—derived from an aura of mountain-like immutability—is the product of the craftsman's meticulous observation and structural composition. It ensures that the artwork not only effectively conveys the thematic essence of the subject matter but also possesses a distinct aesthetic rhythm and order. Equally worthy of mention is the lotus pedestal itself. It features the large, broad-petaled lotus design characteristic of the Pala style, with the petals arranged in a neat, majestic, and rhythmically dynamic pattern. The upper and lower sections are adorned with continuous rows of bead motifs—alternating between large and small—arranged in a progressive, layered sequence. Consequently, the entire lotus pedestal assumes a stepped, trapezoidal structure that visually and structurally reinforces a sense of stability and solemnity. As the esteemed scholar Wang Zhaowen once emphasized, "The sense of volume and mass in sculpture constitutes an intrinsic element that embodies the medium's physicality and aesthetic logic, rather than a mere superficial ostentation of physical size." This particular statue of the God of Wealth perfectly exemplifies this principle; despite its modest dimensions, it conveys—from a visual-psychological perspective—a sense of weightiness and steadfastness no less profound than that of a massive boulder. The craftsman has achieved a rare synthesis of elements: a profound understanding of physical existence, a masterful command of structural form, a keen perception of spatial rhythm, and an unwavering pursuit of the unity between form and spirit.

excerpt.en: A Nepalese image of Vajravidarana, compressed, forceful, and iconographically clear.

viewingNote.zh: 佛教文化衍生出了无数形态各异的雕像，它们或慈眉善目，传递着宁静与祥 和；或威严庄重，彰显着力量与秩序。而当提及“忿怒的护法像”，人们的思绪往 往不由自主地飘向那些目光如炬、血盆大口的雕像。然而，忿怒之下，内心是否 会有一丝惊恐？其实，忿怒也是一种慈悲，忿怒的外表之下，隐藏的是深沉的守 护和对正义的坚守。 忿怒，是一种情绪的表达，但当它雕刻于护法像之上，便成了一种力量的象 征。这尊来自于迦舍末罗王朝的摧破金刚，如一部耐看、有趣的电影，在矛盾的 艺术表现中，带着趣味与深度。 迦舍末罗王朝开启于尼泊尔西部的卡納利盆地，统治者是来自北印度的王族 后裔，他们于 11 世纪末至 12 世纪初进入尼泊尔西部地带，建立了迦舍末罗王 朝，并在 13-14 世纪中叶达到了国力的顶峰。迦舍末罗王朝只存在了两百年左 右，14 世纪末期之后，遭到其他势力的入侵而瓦解。这个王朝虽然短暂，但统 治者笃信佛教，并慷慨地赞助佛教艺术，于是使其获得了极高的艺术成就。 因为笃信佛教，王朝的统治者曾邀请加德满都河谷的纽瓦尔工匠前往迦舍末 罗，在王朝的支持与推动下，将人们创作出了一批批精美的佛教艺术品。但由于 王朝只存在了极短的时间，且加上经历过兵灾战火，导致存世的迦舍末罗王朝造 像极少，据业内学者不完全统计，目前世界范围内已知的、风格明确的迦舍末罗时 期造像作品仅百余件。尽管有幸被传承下来的作品稀少，但这些作品所呈现出的 独立美学风格却令人印象深刻。 摧破金刚在藏传佛教中属于密宗事续，藏名多杰南炯，多杰是“金刚”之 意，“南炯” 为“破坏”之意 。据《金刚摧破根本续》记载，摧破金刚是由世尊 释迦牟尼佛加持金刚手菩萨，使其入于忿怒金刚三摩地而诞生，他总集了一切佛 忿怒、除障、摧破的威力，是金刚手菩萨特殊除障事业的化现。 此尊摧破金刚头戴五骷髅冠，耳旁缯带呈扇形，脸部长满络腮胡，佩戴圆形 耳珰，三目圆鼓，龇牙咧嘴。金刚的形象往往透露着不容侵犯的威严，而此尊摧破金刚的表情，由于面庞浑圆，以及似带微笑的嘴角微扬，反而流露出一种孩童 般的纯真与喜悦。这种表情的塑造，丰富了摧破金刚的形象内涵，结合上身体动 态，使其像是一位在法会上翩翩起舞的舞者，在充满戏剧性张力的同时，既显得 威严又可爱。金刚上身袒露，胸前戴璎珞和骷髅头长链，一条长蛇宛如圣带自左 肩斜披而下，佩戴的天衣从肩膀上落于腰间两侧，随风扬起。臂钏、手环、脚环 以及脚下踏有多条小蛇，在藏传佛教里，蛇象征“愤怒”，这里以蛇作装饰即表示 对“残暴”、“愤怒”的降伏。 右手持金刚杵高高举起，左手持金刚铃，粗旷而有力，肌肉线条分明。下身 披兽皮，兽头垂于两腿之间，刻画逼真；猛兽凶猛易怒，以兽皮为裙，表示着摧 破金刚大无畏的精神以及对嗔怒的降伏。双腿左伸右曲呈弓步站于莲座之上，高 举的右手拉扯着富有弹性的肚皮，仿佛随时就要向下挥动，充满动态感。伸直的 左腿，从胯部顺延至头顶，以及右手在视觉上形成了多条弧线，顺势而上的线 条，形成了一种自然的上升趋势。这些曲线不仅仅将身体轮廓描绘，更是生命力 的象征，它仿佛蕴含着无尽的能量，正蓄势待发，准备冲破一切束缚与阻碍。同 时，强烈的动态感，更是让观者在静谧中感受到一种生生不息的韵律之美。摧破 金刚的塑造细节繁复，但却节奏明确；看似笨重的体态，在艺术家的巧妙构思下 却与轻盈灵动相结合，形成了一种矛盾之下的和谐之美。 在摧破金刚脚踩的莲座正前方有一尊立着的大鹏金翅鸟，在已知的摧破金刚 的造像中与大鹏鸟组合在一起的极为少见，爪子抓住莲台边缘的大鹏鸟，好似正 好把蛇抓回，放置于莲台之上，正在休息的状态。金刚脚下的莲座为单层覆式莲 台，莲台上缘装饰一圈连珠纹，粒粒饱满；莲瓣宽大且圆鼓。在莲台下缘刻有一 行天成体梵文，目前已知带有天城体梵文题记的迦舍末罗造像中，大多是由王室 直接供养，如美国洛杉矶县立博物馆收藏的一尊 14 世纪迦舍末罗空行母（图 一），台座上的铭文译为：“祝愿 Ripumalla 长寿。”根据史料记载迦舍末罗王朝在 国王 Ripumalla 的统治下曾经十分强大，甚至占领了印度的部分领土。这位国王 在公元 1312 年亲自前往佛陀的诞生地蓝毗尼朝圣，并在那里的阿育王石柱上刻下了自己的名字和日期。另外一尊美国华盛顿弗利尔美术馆收藏的 14 世纪迦舍 末 罗 般 若 佛 母 或 供 养 人 像（ 图 二） 中 的 铭 文 译 为：“Dipamalla 造 ”，这 位 Dipamalla 是时任国王 Prthivimalla 的王后，而且根据记载这位国王还曾为大昭 寺捐款修缮过金顶。因此本尊造像上的铭文也应该也和迦舍末罗王室有着千丝 万缕的联系。 此尊摧破金刚的形象塑造展现了雕塑中的矛盾与趣味。他既是一位威严的 护法神祇，又是一位可爱的舞者；他既拥有粗旷的力量，又不失轻盈的灵动。这 种矛盾与趣味并存的特点，不仅让摧破金刚的形象更加立体生动，也赋予了他更 深层次的内涵与深意。不禁让我们思考：在看似对立的事物之间，是否存在着 某种内在的联系与和谐？在力量与美、威严与可爱之间，我们是否能够找到一种 平衡与统一？
viewingNote.en: Buddhist culture has given rise to countless statues of diverse forms—some with benevolent countenances, conveying serenity and harmony; others majestic and solemn, embodying power and order. Yet, when the subject turns to "wrathful guardian deities," the mind often instinctively conjures images of statues with piercing, fiery gazes and gaping, blood-red mouths. However, beneath this wrath, does there perhaps lurk a flicker of fear? In truth, wrath itself is a form of compassion; concealed beneath this furious exterior lies a profound commitment to guardianship and an unwavering adherence to justice. Wrath is, fundamentally, an expression of emotion; yet, when carved into the form of a guardian deity, it transforms into a symbol of power. This particular statue—a depiction of Vajravidaran (the "Destroyer of Obstacles") dating back to the Khasa Malla Kingdom—resembles a compelling and engaging film: through its artistic expression of inherent contradictions, it offers both intrigue and profound depth. The Khasa Malla Kingdom originated in the Karnali Basin of western Nepal. Its rulers were descendants of royal lineages from northern India who entered the western regions of Nepal between the late 11th and early 12th centuries, establishing the kingdom and reaching the zenith of their power between the mid-13th and mid-14th centuries. The Khasa Malla Kingdom existed for only approximately two hundred years, eventually disintegrating after the late 14th century following invasions by rival forces. Although the dynasty was short-lived, its rulers were devout Buddhists who generously patronized Buddhist arts, thereby fostering the attainment of exceptional artistic achievements. Driven by their deep faith, the dynasty's rulers invited Newar artisans from the Kathmandu Valley to Khasa Malla; under the dynasty's patronage and encouragement, these craftsmen created successive waves of exquisite Buddhist artworks. However, due to the dynasty's brief existence—compounded by the ravages of war and conflict—very few Khasa Malla statues have survived to the present day. According to incomplete statistics compiled by scholars in the field, fewer than one hundred works of the Khasa Malla period with clearly identifiable stylistic characteristics are currently known to exist worldwide. Yet, despite the scarcity of these fortunate survivors, the distinct aesthetic style they embody remains profoundly impressive. In Tibetan Buddhism, Vajra Vidaran (the "Destroying Vajra") belongs to the *Kriya Tantra* class of esoteric teachings. Its Tibetan name is *Dorje Namjom*; *Dorje* signifies "Vajra," while *Namjom* means "destruction" or "overcoming." According to the *Root Tantra of Vajra Vidaran*, this deity came into existence when the Lord Buddha Shakyamuni blessed the Bodhisattva Vajrapani, enabling him to enter a state of wrathful Vajra *samadhi*. Vajra Vidaran embodies the collective power of all Buddhas—specifically their wrathful energy, their capacity to dispel obstacles, and their destructive force—and serves as the manifestation of Vajrapani's specialized activity of clearing impediments. This particular depiction of Vajra Vidaran features a crown adorned with five skulls, with ribbon streamers fanning out beside his ears. His face is covered in a full beard, and he wears large circular earrings; his three eyes bulge roundly, and his mouth is twisted in a fierce grimace, baring his fangs. While the imagery of a Vajra deity typically conveys an inviolable majesty, the facial expression of this specific Vajra Vidaran—owing to his rounded features and the slight upward curve of his lips—paradoxically radiates a sense of childlike innocence and joy. This artistic rendering of his expression enriches the iconographic significance of Vajra Vidaran; combined with the dynamic posture of his body, he appears akin to a dancer gracefully performing during a ritual assembly—simultaneously exuding both majesty and charm while maintaining a sense of dramatic tension. The deity’s upper body is bare, adorned with a necklace of jewels and a long garland of skulls; a long serpent drapes diagonally across his chest from his left shoulder, serving as a sacred sash. The celestial scarves (*dhoti*) he wears fall from his shoulders to his waist, fluttering as if caught in the wind. He wears armbands, bracelets, and anklets, and tramples upon several small serpents beneath his feet; in Tibetan Buddhism, the serpent symbolizes "anger," and the use of serpents as ornamentation here signifies the subjugation of "ferocity" and "wrath." His right hand is raised high, brandishing a Vajra scepter, while his left hand holds a Vajra bell; his physique is rugged and powerful, with clearly defined musculature. His lower body is clad in the hide of a wild beast—the animal's head hanging realistically between his legs. Since wild beasts are inherently fierce and prone to rage, wearing such a hide as a skirt symbolizes Vajra Vidaran's fearless spirit and his ultimate conquest over the affliction of anger. Standing in a lunge pose upon a lotus pedestal—with the left leg extended and the right bent—the figure of Vajravidaraṇa holds his right arm aloft, tugging at his taut, elastic belly as if poised to strike downward at any moment; the composition is imbued with a profound sense of dynamism. The extended left leg, the line sweeping upward from the hip to the crown of the head, and the raised right arm visually coalesce into a series of graceful arcs; these ascending lines create a natural, upward-surging momentum. These curves do not merely delineate the contours of the body; they serve as symbols of vital force, appearing to harbor boundless energy—poised and ready to burst forth, shattering all bonds and obstacles. Simultaneously, this intense dynamism allows the viewer to perceive, amidst the stillness of the sculpture, the rhythmic beauty of ceaseless vitality. The sculptural details of Vajravidaraṇa are intricate yet possess a distinct rhythm; his physique, though seemingly heavy and ponderous, is—through the artist's ingenious conception—harmoniously fused with an air of lightness and agility, creating a beauty born of harmonious paradox. Directly in front of the lotus pedestal upon which Vajravidaraṇa stands, a *Garuda* (mythical golden-winged bird) is depicted in an upright posture. Among known Vajravidaraṇa iconography, depictions featuring an accompanying *Garuda* are exceedingly rare. Here, the *Garuda* clutches the rim of the lotus pedestal with its talons, appearing as though it has just captured a serpent, placed it upon the pedestal, and settled into a state of repose. The lotus pedestal beneath Vajravidaraṇa’s feet is a single-tiered, inverted-lotus base; its upper rim is adorned with a band of "continuous pearl" motifs—each bead appearing full and plump—while the lotus petals themselves are broad and convex. Inscribed along the lower rim of the pedestal is a line of Sanskrit text rendered in the Devanagari script. Among known sculptures from the Khasa Malla kingdom that bear Devanagari inscriptions, the majority were commissioned as direct royal offerings—such as the 14th-century Khasa Malla *Dakini* figure housed in the Los Angeles County Museum of Art (Fig. 1), the pedestal inscription of which translates: "May Ripumalla enjoy a long life." Historical records indicate that the Khasa Malla dynasty flourished under the reign of King Ripumalla, growing so powerful that it even annexed portions of Indian territory. In 1312 CE, the King himself undertook a pilgrimage to Lumbini—the birthplace of the Buddha—where he personally inscribed his name and the date upon the Ashoka Pillar located at the site. Another piece—a 14th-century statue of Prajnaparamita or a donor figure from the Khasa Malla kingdom, now housed in the Freer Gallery of Art in Washington, D.C. (Figure 2)—bears an inscription translating to: "Made by Dipamalla." This Dipamalla was the queen consort of the reigning monarch, King Prthivimalla; moreover, historical records indicate that this very king had previously donated funds for the renovation of the golden roof of the Jokhang Temple. Consequently, the inscription found on the present Vajravidaran statue likely also possesses intricate ties to the Khasa Malla royal lineage. The sculptural rendering of this Vajravidaran figure reveals a fascinating interplay of contradictions and artistic charm. He appears simultaneously as a majestic guardian deity and a delightful dancer; he embodies a rugged, raw power yet retains an ethereal lightness and agility. This coexistence of contradictions and charm not only endows the image of Vajravidaran with greater three-dimensionality and vitality but also imbues it with deeper layers of meaning and significance. It compels us to ponder: does an intrinsic connection or harmony exist between seemingly opposing forces? Can we discover a balance and unity between strength and beauty, between majesty and charm?

excerpt.en: A Milarepa image whose austerity is carried by lean structure and lived presence.

viewingNote.zh: 米拉日巴尊者，是藏地家喻户晓的人物。米拉日巴的平生，充满了传奇色 彩，可以说是一首可歌可泣的赞歌。七岁丧父，家产被伯父强行霸占，他与母 亲、妹妹因此受尽了人间饥寒。为报仇雪耻，尊者奉母命拜师修习黑咒，诛杀了 伯父家及其亲属，施降冰雹毁尽了乡邻们的庄稼。尊者自知罪不可恕，为求解脱 希求正法，38 岁时寻访到玛尔巴大师。为了净除罪障，在六年间经受种种苦行 与折磨，终于感动了玛尔巴大师，得到大手印等密法的灌顶和口诀。四十五岁时 因母逝，米拉日巴坚修圣法，开始了在深山长达九年的苦修。不仅证得了噶举派 最高法门大手印成就，而且救度了仇敌与广大众生。之后终生未建道场、未集僧 众，以歌传道的方式游走天涯，做了一位自在洒脱的成就者。 在藏传佛教中，米拉日巴是所有教派都尊崇的大成就者。他有很多身份，如 噶举派第二代祖师、瑜伽大师、苦修者、诗人、道歌大家等等。他曾是一个被仇 恨和欲念蒙蔽双眼的人，但他用自己的一生，诠释了“命运多舛、涅槃重生”的 深刻含义，让世人相信，无论过往如何，都有机会通过不懈的努力与内心的觉 醒，实现涅槃重生。这不仅是米拉日巴个人的传奇，更是对人性光辉与生命力量 的颂歌。米拉日巴真正体现了佛教初始时期重视实践的精神，使逐渐理论化、形 式化的佛教复活了原有的生命力。米拉日巴一生都在踐行原始佛教朴实艰苦的 实践精神，在深山中刻苦修行時，每日只以荨麻度日，全身泛绿，形如枯搞。匠 人在雕刻此件作品的選材時就有特別考量，選取了西藏本土色澤黑中透绿的地方 玉材质，完美的与尊者在山洞苦修時的飲食与肤色达到契合。 此件石雕造像对米拉日巴苦修形象进行了最真实的写实还原。尊者披头散 发，面容消瘦，额头青筋爆起，颧骨突出，双目炯炯，眼白和瞳孔保留下来的原彩 彩绘更是让其眼神散发光彩与坚毅。尊者相貌奇古，却含蓄浑成，所以趣味隽 永，耐人晤对。当时西藏本土的美学风格以理想化的人物写真、佛、菩萨、护法 造像为主，对于不以传统形式为依据的造像雕刻是少之又少，需要匠人发挥足够 的艺术天分。 尊者身躯瘦骨嶙峋，胸骨与脊椎骨明显突出，分明可数的脊肋，传达着苦修的忘我情怀。这种细腻气质的抓取与表达需要匠人对人体结构、骨骼比例、以及 解剖的深度研究。尊者身躯瘦骨嶙峋，胸骨与脊椎骨明显突出，右手高举耳 边，这动态好似在唱诵道歌，又似在聆听众生之言。左手结触地印，双腿细瘦结 游戏姿坐于铺着兽皮的台座上，台座设计极简。坚毅的眼神、消瘦的身体，透着 一种坚毅与韧性，与之对视似乎能感受到其命运多舛、涅槃重生的坚定与智慧。 在塑造一件细节很多的雕塑时，往往很容易塑造得零碎而失去整体感。但 这一件苦修像虽然刻画的细节很多，不论是头颈肩的关系，骨骼或者肌肉的走 向，以及眼部塑造等等，是相互融合在整个体块当中，且融洽自然，并没有破坏 掉整体性的节奏。所以当我们走近或离远看的时候，会感觉这件雕塑有一股充 盈的气在围绕包裹。加上表面黝黑的材质，使整个人物气质非常的沉稳、充 盈，且充满真实的血肉感。可以联想到当时刻画这件作品的匠人，应当是一位极 具思想与天分的优秀艺术家。石质造像从材质属性来说，是一种做减法的雕刻 过程，容错率极低，稍微失误就得重来，且不易保存，再传承历经几百年几十代 人的守护得以完好保存下来的石质雕像有着难以言喻的珍贵。在已知的米拉日 巴石雕苦修像中多以西藏流行的黄蜡石雕刻的在岩洞中修行的小型随色造像为主 ，像此尊雕刻如此细腻、传神的大尺寸米拉日巴苦修石雕造像是极为少 见，相似的铜造像可参考拉萨西藏博物馆收藏的米拉日巴像。
viewingNote.en: The Venerable Milarepa is a household name throughout the Tibetan lands. Milarepa’s life story is steeped in legend—it is, in essence, a stirring and heroic epic. At the age of seven, he lost his father, and his family’s entire estate was forcibly seized by his uncle; consequently, he, his mother, and his sister endured the utmost deprivation, suffering the bitter pangs of hunger and cold. Driven by a mandate from his mother to avenge their wrongs and redeem their family honor, the Venerable One sought out a master to learn the dark arts of sorcery. Through these powers, he brought about the deaths of his uncle’s entire household and their kin, and subsequently summoned a devastating hailstorm that utterly destroyed the crops of his neighbors. Recognizing the unforgivable nature of his transgressions, and yearning for liberation through the true Dharma, he sought out the Great Master Marpa at the age of thirty-eight. To purify his karmic obstructions, he endured six years of rigorous asceticism and grueling trials; his unwavering perseverance finally moved Master Marpa, who then bestowed upon him the empowerments and oral instructions for profound esoteric teachings, such as Mahamudra. At the age of forty-five—following the passing of his mother—Milarepa dedicated himself exclusively to the practice of the sacred Dharma, embarking on a solitary retreat of intense asceticism deep within the mountains that would last for nine years. During this period, he not only attained the ultimate realization of Mahamudra—the highest teaching of the Kagyu lineage—but also extended his compassion to liberate both his former enemies and sentient beings at large. Thereafter, for the remainder of his life, he neither established a monastic center nor gathered a formal congregation; instead, he wandered across the land as a solitary pilgrim, conveying the Dharma through the medium of song—living as a truly free-spirited and unencumbered realized master. Within the tradition of Tibetan Buddhism, Milarepa is revered by all schools and lineages as a Great Accomplished Master. He is known by many titles: the Second Patriarch of the Kagyu lineage, a master yogi, a supreme ascetic, a poet, and a preeminent composer of spiritual songs. Though he was once a man whose vision was blinded by hatred and worldly desires, he dedicated his entire life to embodying the profound truth that "adversity can lead to spiritual rebirth." Through his example, he demonstrated to the world that—regardless of one's past transgressions—it is always possible, through unceasing effort and inner awakening, to achieve spiritual liberation and renewal. Milarepa’s story is not merely the legend of a single individual; it stands as a magnificent ode to the inherent radiance of human nature and the indomitable power of the human spirit. He truly embodied the spirit of practical application and direct experience that characterized early Buddhism, thereby revitalizing the original vitality of the Dharma at a time when it was becoming increasingly theoretical and formalized. Throughout his life, Milarepa embodied the austere and rigorous ascetic spirit of early Buddhism; while undergoing arduous spiritual training deep within the mountains, he subsisted solely on nettles, causing his skin to turn green and his body to become gaunt and withered. In selecting the material for this piece, the craftsman exercised particular care, choosing a local Tibetan jade—characterized by a dark hue with subtle green undertones—that perfectly harmonizes with the Venerable One's diet and complexion during his solitary asceticism in the mountain caves. This stone sculpture offers a remarkably authentic and realistic portrayal of Milarepa in his state of ascetic self-denial. The Venerable One is depicted with disheveled, flowing hair, a gaunt countenance, bulging veins on his forehead, and prominent cheekbones; his eyes—bright and piercing—are further enlivened by the preserved original pigments in the whites and pupils, imbuing his gaze with both radiance and unwavering resolve. Though his appearance is strikingly unconventional, it possesses a subtle yet cohesive harmony, creating a timeless aesthetic appeal that invites prolonged contemplation. At the time this work was created, the prevailing aesthetic in Tibet favored idealized representations of figures—primarily Buddhas, Bodhisattvas, and Dharma Protectors; sculptures that deviated from these traditional iconographic norms were exceedingly rare, demanding exceptional artistic talent and ingenuity from the craftsman. The Venerable One’s body is skeletal and emaciated, with his sternum and spine protruding distinctly and his ribs clearly visible—a visual testament to the selfless devotion inherent in his ascetic practice. Capturing and conveying such nuanced physical characteristics required the craftsman to possess a profound understanding of human anatomy, skeletal structure, and physiological proportions. With his gaunt frame and protruding bones, the Venerable One raises his right hand high toward his ear—a dynamic gesture suggesting he is either chanting a spiritual song or listening intently to the voices of sentient beings. His left hand forms the *Bhumisparsha Mudra* (Earth-Touching Gesture), while his slender legs are crossed in a relaxed posture atop a pedestal draped with an animal hide—a pedestal designed with striking minimalism. His resolute gaze and emaciated physique radiate an aura of unyielding determination and resilience; to meet his eyes is to sense the profound wisdom and steadfast resolve with which he navigated a life fraught with adversity, ultimately achieving spiritual rebirth and Nirvana. When crafting a sculpture rich in intricate details, there is often a risk that the work may become fragmented, thereby losing its overall sense of aesthetic unity and cohesion. Although this sculpture of Milarepa in ascetic practice features a wealth of intricate details—ranging from the interplay of the head, neck, and shoulders to the contours of the bones and muscles, as well as the rendering of the eyes—these elements are seamlessly integrated into the overall mass of the form, harmonizing naturally without disrupting the rhythmic integrity of the whole. Consequently, whether viewed from up close or from a distance, the sculpture conveys a palpable sense of vital energy—a *qi*—that seems to envelop and permeate the figure. Enhanced by the dark, lustrous quality of its material, the figure exudes an aura of profound composure and fullness, imbued with a vivid sense of living flesh and blood. One is led to surmise that the artisan who crafted this piece must have been an exceptional artist—a master of both profound intellect and extraordinary talent. From a material perspective, stone sculpture is an inherently subtractive process; it allows for virtually no margin of error—even a minor slip necessitates starting the work anew—and is inherently difficult to preserve. Thus, a stone statue that has survived intact through the guardianship of countless generations over hundreds of years possesses a preciousness that defies description. Among the known stone sculptures of Milarepa in ascetic practice, the majority consist of small-scale, color-enhanced figures—typically carved from the "yellow wax stone" popular in Tibet—depicting the sage meditating within a cave. A large-scale stone sculpture such as this one—executed with such exquisite detail and lifelike expressiveness—is exceedingly rare; for a comparable example in bronze, one might look to the statue of Milarepa housed in the Tibet Museum in Lhasa.

label.en: Formerly in the collection of Rossi & Rossi

excerpt.en: A Pala-period Acala carved in stone with concentrated wrathful force.

viewingNote.zh: 帕拉王朝，这个 位于印度东北部的王朝，以其对佛教的尊崇和艺术创造 力，成为了印度佛教史上一个不可磨灭的印记。帕拉王朝时期，佛教密宗得到了 前所未有的尊崇与推广。历代君王都是佛教的忠实信徒和积极推广者，他们不仅 在国内大力弘扬佛法，还通过踊跃的外交方式将佛教传播到周边国家和地区。其 中，孟加拉高僧阿底峡的入藏弘法便是帕拉王朝佛教文化交流的一个重要例证。 这一时期的佛教，不仅得到了国家的全力支持与赞助，还吸引了众多高僧大德前 来传法授业。 十到十二世纪期间，菩提伽耶作为帕拉王朝的宗教中心孕育了众多的造像作 品。除了供奉于寺庙内的大型雕像以外，还制作了尺寸较小、工艺精湛的铜像和 石雕造像。这些作品大多出自菩提伽耶大正觉寺周边的工坊，其制作目的主要是 便于携带，以便于朝圣者在朝拜圣地时请购，并将之带往各地供奉。本尊不动明 王随身像便是此类造像中的一件典型代表。 不动明王，又称不动尊菩萨，源自佛教密宗体系，是密教明王之主首，也是佛 教中重要的护法神之一。他的名字源自于坚定的誓言，即保护佛法不受任何邪恶 势力的侵犯。由黄蜡石雕刻而成的这尊不动明王，第一眼给人古朴的质感，很符 合石雕的语言。不动明王一面两臂，头顶 发髻高耸，佩 戴 小三叶冠。三目圆 睁，鼻型宽厚；上裂的嘴角露出一排整齐的牙齿，凶狠、恐怖，咬紧着牙根的面部 肌肉臌胀，现出大忿怒相。上身赤祼，胸前戴项圈，一条生索自左肩斜披而下。 右手弯曲上举持宝剑，象征着斩断除嗔、怒、慢三毒，以及一切邪恶和障碍 的力量。左手当胸作期克印持套索，套索在肩头打结，似乎随时准备降伏妖魔。 不动明王身体向左前倾，双腿呈左侧跪姿，此种半跪姿的不动明王形象为早期图 像风格。不动明王下身着裙裤，裙裤上雕刻有璎珞为装饰，腰带上方的挤出来的 小肚腩，生动写实，具有鲜活的生命力气息。胸腔到胯部、腿部、以及脚趾的流畅线条具有很强的连接性，会让人忘记石质的坚硬和顿感，随之而来的是一种 轻盈和柔软的视觉感官，犹如一位在舞动的舞者，将为正义而舞的那一瞬间定格 在了这件石雕之上。 不动明王背后为雕刻有火焰纹的极简背光，下方莲台为宽阔厚实的双层仰 覆式莲瓣，莲瓣排列规整，饱满中带着莲瓣厚实的肉质感。在莲台左下方有一 位跪着双手合十的供养人形象，供养人的出现增添了作品的烟火气息和宗教意 味，在已知的帕拉王朝石雕造像中此种表现形式较为少见。匠人将其不容任何 邪恶力量侵犯的威严感定格在了一件只有八厘米的石雕上，使得这件作品在微小 的尺寸中蕴含着巨大的力量。一件能随身携带的石雕从诞生之初，被匠人赋予 了生命力与灵魂之后，然而，石雕的命运却并非一定会一帆风顺，在近千年的时 光里，会经历无数的风霜雨雪、人为无意或有意的破坏或者忽视，每一次历史的 变迁、每一次人为的侵扰，都可能对它造成不可逆转的损害，然而，这件石雕却 无比幸运地挺过了这一切，依然保持着原有的状态和神韵，这背后，是多少代 人、多少年的悉心守护！
viewingNote.en: The Pala Dynasty, situated in northeastern India, left an indelible mark on the history of Indian Buddhism through its profound reverence for the faith and its remarkable artistic creativity. During the Pala era, Esoteric Buddhism received unprecedented veneration and promotion. The dynasty's successive monarchs were devout believers and active proponents of Buddhism; they not only vigorously propagated the Dharma within their own realm but also actively disseminated it to neighboring countries and regions through dynamic diplomatic initiatives. A prime example of this Buddhist cultural exchange during the Pala period is the journey of the eminent Bengali monk Atisha to Tibet to propagate the Dharma. During this era, Buddhism enjoyed not only the full support and patronage of the state but also attracted numerous eminent monks and masters who traveled there to teach and impart their wisdom. Between the 10th and 12th centuries, Bodh Gaya—serving as the religious epicenter of the Pala Dynasty—became a prolific hub for the creation of Buddhist imagery. In addition to the monumental statues enshrined within temples, artisans also produced smaller, exquisitely crafted bronze and stone sculptures. Most of these works originated in workshops surrounding the Mahabodhi Temple in Bodh Gaya; they were primarily designed to be portable, allowing pilgrims visiting the holy site to purchase them and carry them back to their respective regions for veneration. The portable image of the deity Acala (Fudō Myōō) featured here stands as a quintessential example of this category of sculpture. Acala—also known as Acalanatha Bodhisattva—originates from the pantheon of Esoteric Buddhism; he is the foremost among the Esoteric Wisdom Kings (*Vidyarajas*) and ranks as one of the most significant Dharma protectors within the Buddhist tradition. His name derives from his unwavering vow to safeguard the Dharma against encroachment by any malevolent forces. Carved from yellow waxstone, this particular image of Acala immediately conveys a sense of rustic antiquity that perfectly complements the inherent aesthetic language of stone sculpture. Acala is depicted with a single face and two arms; his hair is gathered into a towering topknot surmounted by a small, three-lobed crown. His three eyes stare wide open, and his nose is broad and substantial; the corners of his mouth curl upward to reveal a row of clenched, even teeth—a fierce and terrifying expression further intensified by the bulging facial muscles that betray his state of supreme wrath. His upper torso is bare, adorned with a pectoral collar, while a ritual cord (*upavita*) draped in the form of a living serpent descends diagonally across his chest from his left shoulder. The right arm is bent and raised upward, holding a sword; this symbolizes the power to sever and eradicate the "three poisons"—anger, pride, and delusion—as well as all forms of evil and obstruction. The left hand is held at chest level, forming the *Tarjani Mudra* (warning gesture) while grasping a lasso; the lasso is knotted over the shoulder, appearing poised to subdue demons at any moment. Fudo Myoo’s body leans forward and to the left, with his legs positioned in a kneeling posture on the left side; this specific semi-kneeling iconography represents an early stylistic convention. Fudo Myoo’s lower body is clad in a skirt-like garment adorned with carved *yignog* (beaded garlands); the slight bulge of the belly, visible just above the waistband, is rendered with vivid realism, imbuing the figure with a palpable sense of vitality. The fluid lines flowing from the chest down through the hips, legs, and toes create a strong sense of visual continuity, causing the viewer to forget the inherent hardness and inertness of the stone medium. Instead, one perceives a visual lightness and softness—as if capturing the very instant a dancer, moving in the service of justice, has been frozen in time within this stone sculpture. Behind Fudo Myoo rises a minimalist *mandorla* (halo) carved with flame motifs, while the lotus pedestal beneath consists of broad, substantial double-tiered petals arranged in an alternating upward-and-downward pattern. These petals are aligned with perfect regularity, appearing full-bodied and conveying the fleshy, organic texture characteristic of lotus blossoms. Positioned at the lower left of the pedestal is the figure of a kneeling donor with hands clasped in *anjali mudra* (prayer). The inclusion of this donor figure adds a touch of human warmth and heightened religious significance to the work—a compositional feature that is relatively rare among known stone sculptures from the Pala Dynasty. The artisan has successfully captured Fudo Myoo’s majestic aura—one that brooks no intrusion by any evil force—within a stone carving measuring a mere eight centimeters, thereby endowing this diminutive masterpiece with a monumental sense of power. From the very moment of its creation—when a craftsman first breathed life and soul into this portable stone carving—its destiny was by no means guaranteed to be a smooth one. Over the span of nearly a thousand years, it has endured countless trials: the ravages of wind, frost, rain, and snow; and the unintentional or deliberate acts of human destruction and neglect. Every historical upheaval, every human intrusion, carried the potential to inflict irreversible damage upon it. Yet, with extraordinary fortune, this stone carving has weathered it all, retaining its original form and spirit to this day—a testament to the meticulous guardianship of generations of people over the course of centuries.

excerpt.en: A gilt-bronze portrait of a mahasiddha, alert, wiry, and inwardly charged.

viewingNote.zh: 在喜马拉雅这片古老而神秘的大地区，孕育着一种独特的精神追求与修行方 式——瑜伽。瑜伽士，作为这一古老智慧的传承者与实践者，以其独特的修行路 径，展现了超越常人的智慧与洞见。瑜伽士将精神层面的成长与觉醒视为生命的 终极目标。他们相信，真正的幸福与满足并非来自外界的给予，而是源自内心的 平和与智慧。因此，他们选择了一条少有人走的路，放弃了对物质世界的过度追 求，转而投身于精神世界的探索与修行。 他们的行为，在外人眼中或许显得难以理解甚至荒谬，他们可能长时间保持 某个姿势不动、进行极端的呼吸控制或沉浸在长时间的冥想之中。然而，正是这 些看似不合常理的举止，成为了瑜伽士实现精神觉醒与智慧寻求的重要途径。他 们通过不断的修行与探索，逐渐超越了肉体的限制与世俗的束缚，达到了心灵的 自由与解脱。这种自我实现的过程艰辛而漫长。 大成就者，指金刚乘佛教中那些修行佛教密宗瑜伽，特别是无上瑜伽密续而 获得成就的瑜伽士。他们在 6~10 世纪印度无上瑜伽密续兴起的过程中扮演了十 分关键的角色。在佛教文化数百年的传颂和演绎中，这些修行者们受到了广泛地 崇拜。他们所作的种种修法要门和原典解论构成了藏传佛教无上瑜伽密修持最 为基本的理论体系，对大乘佛教后期的发展做出了至关重要的贡献。藏传佛教 中对上师地位的崇敬与重视，与对大成就者的崇拜密不可分，甚至可以说是大成 就者信仰的延续和升华。 大成就者这些写真类的人物肖像他们大多都是历史上一位位真实存在过的 人物。他们看上去如此鲜活的性格体现离不开写真的魅力。此尊于西藏 13 世纪 左右制作的成就者肖像五官塑造突出骨骼感，尤其是眉骨转折锋利。眉弓下双眼 炯炯、澄澈，仿佛仍在凝视着无尽的虚空，那是一种超越了物质世界的深邃与宁 静，是相由心生的外在呈现。鼻骨硬挺、颧骨突出，微微上扬的唇角，似笑又似悲悯；唇线转折干脆利落，下巴饱满有力。从面部塑造可看出人物形象的坚毅与 智慧，是一种隐士高僧的气质，像是在传达着智者一生对心的修行和锤炼。高耸 的发髻部分披散肩部，垂落于肩部的圆形耳饰，具有典型的时代图像学特征。披 戴禅修带的身姿轻盈、舒展。以点连成线的腰带内装饰有不同随型形状，此种装 饰方式与帕拉王朝时期美学风格相融，同样具备典型时代风格特征（参阅图一）。 智者于胸前结说法印，结印的双手与身体形成了视觉上前后虚实的空间感，鲜活 的说法画面似乎跃然眼前。在佛教中说法印象征着传授智慧与法门的庄重与神 圣，结印的双手与体态是如此的契合，灵动且柔软。结全跏趺坐的腿部肌肉结 实、修长且比例合度，散发着一种坚定、沉稳的力量。 种种细节的塑造构成了通往人物灵魂的桥梁，此尊外表看似简单、单纯的肖 像，实则蕴含着更多关于大成就者丰富的精神世界秘境与耐看的神韵。
viewingNote.en: Within the ancient and mysterious expanse of the Himalayas, a unique spiritual pursuit and practice—Yoga—took root. Yogis, as the custodians and practitioners of this ancient wisdom, demonstrate a wisdom and insight that transcend the ordinary through their distinctive paths of spiritual discipline. Yogis regard spiritual growth and awakening as the ultimate objective of life. They believe that true happiness and contentment do not stem from external endowments, but rather originate from inner peace and wisdom. Consequently, they have chosen a path less traveled, renouncing the excessive pursuit of the material world to instead dedicate themselves to the exploration and practice of the spiritual realm. To outsiders, their actions may appear incomprehensible—or even absurd; they may remain motionless in a single posture for extended periods, engage in extreme breath control, or immerse themselves in prolonged meditation. Yet, it is precisely these seemingly unconventional behaviors that serve as the vital conduits through which Yogis attain spiritual awakening and pursue wisdom. Through ceaseless practice and exploration, they gradually transcend the limitations of the physical body and the fetters of the mundane world, thereby attaining spiritual freedom and liberation. This process of self-realization is both arduous and protracted. The *Mahasiddhas*—or "Great Adepts"—refer to those Yogis within Vajrayana Buddhism who, through the practice of Buddhist Tantric Yoga (specifically the *Anuttarayoga Tantras*), attained spiritual realization. They played a pivotal role during the period between the 6th and 10th centuries, when the *Anuttarayoga Tantras* were rising to prominence in India. Over centuries of transmission and interpretation within Buddhist culture, these practitioners have been widely revered. The various methods of practice and exegetical commentaries on original texts that they authored constitute the foundational theoretical framework for the practice of *Anuttarayoga Tantra* within Tibetan Buddhism, making a critical contribution to the subsequent development of Mahayana Buddhism. The reverence and importance accorded to the *Guru* (spiritual master) within Tibetan Buddhism are inextricably linked to the veneration of the *Mahasiddhas*—indeed, this reverence may be viewed as both a continuation and a sublimation of the faith originally placed in the Great Adepts. The portraits of the *Mahasiddhas*—rendered in a vivid, true-to-life style—depict figures who were, for the most part, actual historical personages. The vividness with which their distinct personalities are captured owes much to the compelling power of this realistic artistic tradition. This portrait of a *Mahasiddha* (Great Adept), created in Tibet around the 13th century, features facial modeling that emphasizes skeletal structure—most notably the sharply defined contours of the brow ridges. Beneath these arches, the eyes appear bright and limpid, as if still gazing into the boundless void; this gaze conveys a profound serenity that transcends the material world—an external manifestation of the Buddhist principle that "form is born of the mind." The bridge of the nose is firm and prominent, the cheekbones high; the corners of the lips curl slightly upward, suggesting a smile yet also a sense of deep compassion. The lines of the lips are crisply delineated, and the chin appears full and strong. The facial modeling reveals the figure's unwavering resolve and wisdom—the very aura of a hermit-monk or high-ranking prelate—seemingly conveying the lifetime of spiritual cultivation and mental discipline undertaken by such a sage. The towering topknot of hair is partially unbound, cascading over the shoulders, where it meets a pair of circular earrings—iconographic details typical of this specific historical period. The figure, draped in a meditation sash, assumes a posture that appears both light and expansive. The waist sash—composed of a linear series of dots enclosing various free-form shapes—reflects an ornamental style harmoniously blended with the aesthetic traditions of the Pala Dynasty, thereby exhibiting stylistic characteristics typical of that era (see Figure 1). The sage holds his hands at chest level in the *Dharmachakra Mudra* (Gesture of Turning the Wheel of Dharma); the interplay between the hands and the body creates a visual sense of spatial depth—of foreground and background, of solid form and void—bringing the vivid scene of the Buddha's discourse to life before the viewer's eyes. In Buddhist iconography, this gesture symbolizes the solemn and sacred act of imparting wisdom and spiritual teachings; the positioning of the hands and the overall bodily posture are harmoniously integrated, appearing at once fluid and supple. Seated in the full lotus position (*padmasana*), the figure’s legs display firm, slender musculature and balanced proportions, radiating an aura of steadfast, grounded strength. The meticulous rendering of these myriad details serves as a bridge leading to the very soul of the subject. Thus, while this portrait of a *Mahasiddha* may appear outwardly simple and unadorned, it in fact encapsulates the rich, mystical inner world of a Great Adept—possessing a profound spiritual resonance that rewards prolonged contemplation.

label.zh: 2004年9月 纽约苏富比 lot 62号

excerpt.en: A Gandharan standing Buddha with quiet frontality and architectural drapery.

viewingNote.zh: “犍陀罗”一个历史上非常重要且充满文化魅力的名字。 这个名字，或许在现代已经不再是广泛流传的地理名词，但在佛教传播、艺术 演化乃至文明交流的历史长河中，它所留下的印记，至今依然清晰深刻 。犍陀 罗，不只是一个地域 ，更是一种象征——东西文明在此相遇、融合、重构，影响着 从印度河到长安的精神之路。 古代犍陀罗国位于现今巴基斯坦西北部与阿富汗东部交界处，是东西交通的 要冲，也是丝绸之路的节点，既连接印度河流域 ，又通向中亚和波斯。王国的核心 区域包括：塔克希拉、白沙瓦 、斯瓦特河谷、喀布尔河流域 。由于地理优势，犍陀 罗也就天然地成为了宗教、文化 、艺术、思想、语言等多重交流的汇集点 。 自阿育王时代（公元前 3 世纪）起，犍陀罗成为了佛教传播的重镇，在大乘佛 教兴起中也起到了关键的作用，许多重要的佛教经典 、译师与宗派都源于这里 。 同样，犍陀罗是一个文化交融的创新区域 。在此之前，佛陀的形象多以法轮、菩 提树、空座等象征符号来体现，犍陀罗艺术打破了传统 ，在融合了西方雕刻技法与 写实主义精神的基础上，创作出了佛陀的具象形象 。这种艺术语言，正是来自亚历 山大东征与希腊巴克特里亚王国建立后，希腊的雕刻技法以及神祇造型，对当地艺 术的渗入 。那犍陀罗艺术的形成，也影响到了中国的云岗、龙门、麦积山等佛教石 窟造像，构成了东传佛教艺术的母体之一。 在那片土地上，来自阿契美尼德与萨珊波斯的建筑与装饰风格，还有印度本土 传统的服饰与神话故事、宗教理念，以及贵霜帝国时期的受中亚游牧影响的草原 文化、语言与贸易系统也都被纳入其中，使其成为了南亚次大陆古代文明中一个除 了地理以外，在文化意义上也同样重要的王国。伫立在我们眼前的这件佛陀像就 诞生于公元 3-4 世纪的犍陀罗。佛陀面容平和、身姿优雅、比例和谐、衣褶流畅，兼并一种理性的秩序与感 性的柔情之美 。受到希腊艺术影响的佛陀一头波浪卷发，身穿通肩袈裟 。流畅 的面部轮廓内是深邃 、立体的五官；含蓄的笑意中潜藏着东方特有的内敛和温 柔 。一手拿着衣角、一脚微微弯曲的体态，有种向我们徐徐走来的动势。佛陀 身穿的通肩式袈裟层次明朗、线条清晰，虽质感略显厚重，但依旧能看到宽阔 的骨骼和健康的体魄 。这种状态的散发是写实主义与理想主义精神的表达 。虽 然匠人将精神之美、人性之美，凝固在了每一刀的刻画中，但这种美并非单一。 它是多元的、流动的，是匠人的参与构建，也是可被观者重新解读的。 在犍陀罗艺术的雕塑题材中，佛陀像和佛传故事是雕刻的核心主题 。如果 仅仅只是乍一看，许多佛陀造像似乎面貌相似，仿佛千篇一律；但如果驻足细 看，就会发现每一尊的雕刻都蕴含着微妙的差异：有的温柔慈悲、有的庄严挺 拔、有的更显人性、有的则更趋神性 。这些差别，是不同匠人对“理想”各自的 诠释，也是他们内在精神和技艺的投射。而我，作为站在雕塑前的观者，也在 经历一场“对镜”的转换 。 正伫立在我眼前的这尊佛陀像，俊朗、优美、平和且生动。记得第一次面 对这尊雕塑，我正看得投入时，竟下意识地整理了一下自己的衣着。那一刻我在 问自己：我是否也能如此优雅 ？更确切地说，我是否能在“文化的理想”中受 到滋养，成为一个更好的人？那一刻，审美触发了自我意识的觉醒，我像是在他 者的理想化映照中看到自己尚未抵达的状态 。 临近展览需要撰写内容，我又再次驻足于这件雕塑面前，触摸祂的轮 廓 、感受祂情绪的散发 。但这一次，我所感受到的“理想”发生了变化 。也许 在一些人看来，这次我的关注点略显奇怪，但这正是一件充满耐看性的作品所 具有的魅力——它总能启发出新的思考与感受 。这次的“对镜”，我竟被佛陀那 隆起的小肚子安抚了焦虑：原来，即便是如此美好的理想化形象，也不曾掩饰 身体的真实与自然 。 我不禁联想到自古以来社会通过“审美”对身体的规训。这种控制并不通 过暴力，而是通过审美趣味、媒体 、以及所谓美的标准等等悄然进行，让个体 在看似自由的选择中，完成了自我规训。在我们当今的社会中，精英阶层追求肌 肉线条、纯素饮食、精致养护，背后是消费能力的体现；而大部分普通人的身体 却往往被污名化，比如胖、黑 、邋遢、不时尚……这些标签不仅揭示了阶级偏 见，也迫使了很多人哪怕以牺牲健康、压迫自然为代价，也要符合“美的标 准”，这是一种审美霸权，它用单一的标准压制了其他美的可能性，制造出了普 遍的身材焦虑与深层的自我异化，而曾经的我，也不例外。 在消费主义社会中，美不再只是感性的经验，而被转化为消费目标与市场 逻辑 。美成了一种权力结构的展演：越接近主流审美的人体，越容易获得文化 认同与社会资源 。我们渐渐忘了，美的本质究竟是什么？一个人“真正美的样 子”，又该如何理解？ 理查德 · 舒斯特曼在《身体美学》中曾指出：“身体不是展示美的工具，而 是体验、表达与生成美的核心媒介。”美不仅是外表的和谐，更多的是一种由 个体内在心理 、精神面貌、情绪能量所散发出的综合魅力。它既不固定于某一 具象形式，也不取决于外部评价标准，而是一种精神状态的感性显现 。它是一 种随时间、情绪、境遇、意识流动而变化的动态生成状态 。不是“成为什么样 子”，而是“正在那样地存在”。这就像当我们谈论这尊犍陀罗雕塑的美时，我们 其实不是在谈“形体多完美”，而是在谈祂如何通过“非语言”的方式传递出的 宁静、平和、智慧与包容的气质，这种气质，是可感的、是动人的、是健康的。 然而，在自媒体与资本共同塑造的语境中，审美标准愈发单一。美的本质 应该是多元的、开放的、状态性的。写到这里，我忽然想起在巴黎卢浮宫看展 时的一幕，一位解说员正情绪饱满的和团队成员介绍着馆内的雕塑，恰巧路过 的我听见他说：“这是女人最美的样子。”我忍不住好奇，回头一看，那是用大 理石雕刻的一件罗马时期的女性人体雕塑 。她的身形并不纤瘦，手臂、腰 间、臀部都有赘肉，也就是今天我们所说的“梨形身材”。然而，那种从容与真 实，反而比当下的“标准美”更打动我 。我们常说，美是感受到的，而非算出 来的。但我们似乎忘了——所有感受的起点，就是身体本身。 身体的状态，其实早已决定了我们与世界交往的方式 。疲惫的身体，自然 难以散发明亮的气质；松弛安定的身体，不必多言就已传达出了一种静能生慧 的力量；当我们看到一位中年人的笑容时，虽然脸上有皱纹，但当我们被他（她） 内心的广阔与温润而打动时，这种魅力是无法被“标准审美”所量化，这就是“美 不是外形，而是一种状态”的根本所在 。 所以，当我站在这尊犍陀罗雕塑面前时，我看见的不只是雕塑之美，更是 一种重新理解“美”的可能 。它打破了我对完美主义的幻象，让我体会到真 实、健康、自然的状态，并由此反思当代社会对身体的压迫 。理想，不应成为 现实的对立面，而应是我们理解自身、接纳他人、欣赏世界的起点 。美，不在于 “变成什么样”，而在于“如其所是”的自在存在 。
viewingNote.en: "Gandhara"—a name of immense historical significance and profound cultural allure. While this name may no longer be a widely recognized geographical term in the modern era, the imprint it has left upon the long currents of history—spanning the dissemination of Buddhism, the evolution of art, and the exchange of civilizations—remains as vivid and profound today as ever. Gandhara is not merely a geographical region; it is, above all, a symbol—a place where Eastern and Western civilizations met, merged, and were reimagined, thereby influencing the spiritual pathways stretching from the Indus River to Chang'an. The ancient Kingdom of Gandhara was situated at the crossroads of present-day northwestern Pakistan and eastern Afghanistan; serving as a vital strategic nexus for East-West transit and a key node on the Silk Road, it connected the Indus River Basin while simultaneously opening routes toward Central Asia and Persia. The kingdom's core territories encompassed Taxila, Peshawar, the Swat Valley, and the Kabul River Basin. By virtue of its advantageous geographical position, Gandhara naturally evolved into a vibrant confluence for the exchange of religion, culture, art, philosophy, and language. From the era of Emperor Ashoka (3rd century BCE) onward, Gandhara emerged as a pivotal center for the propagation of Buddhism. It played a critical role in the rise of Mahayana Buddhism; indeed, numerous seminal Buddhist scriptures, renowned translators, and distinct schools of thought trace their origins back to this very region. Furthermore, Gandhara distinguished itself as a dynamic zone of cultural synthesis and artistic innovation. Prior to this period, the image of the Buddha was typically represented through symbolic motifs—such as the Dharma Wheel, the Bodhi Tree, or the Empty Throne. Gandhara art, however, broke with these traditions; by fusing Western sculptural techniques with a spirit of realism, it pioneered the creation of the Buddha's anthropomorphic, or figurative, image. This distinctive artistic idiom was the direct result of the infiltration of Greek sculptural techniques and iconographic styles—introduced following the Eastern campaigns of Alexander the Great and the subsequent establishment of the Greco-Bactrian Kingdom—into the local artistic landscape. Consequently, the formation of Gandhara art exerted a profound influence upon the Buddhist cave sculptures found at sites across China—such as Yungang, Longmen, and Maijishan—thereby serving as one of the foundational wellsprings for the Buddhist art that spread eastward. In that land, architectural and decorative styles from the Achaemenid and Sassanid Persian empires—along with indigenous Indian traditions regarding attire, mythology, and religious philosophy—were seamlessly integrated. Furthermore, during the era of the Kushan Empire, the steppe culture, languages, and trade networks influenced by Central Asian nomads were also incorporated into this milieu. Consequently, this realm emerged as a kingdom of profound significance within the ancient civilizations of the South Asian subcontinent—a significance extending far beyond mere geography to encompass a rich and vital cultural identity. The statue of the Buddha standing before us today was created in Gandhara during the 3rd or 4th century CE. The Buddha’s countenance is serene, his posture elegant, his proportions harmonious, and the folds of his robes flow with grace; he embodies a beauty that harmonizes rational order with tender sensibility. Influenced by Greek artistic traditions, the Buddha features a head of wavy curls and is clad in a monastic robe draped over both shoulders. Within the fluid contours of his face lie deep-set, three-dimensional features; a subtle smile conceals a sense of restraint and gentleness characteristic of the East. His posture—one hand holding the edge of his robe, one leg slightly bent—creates a dynamic impression, as if he were slowly walking toward us. The monastic robe draped over both shoulders is rendered with distinct layers and crisp lines; although its texture appears somewhat heavy, it nonetheless reveals the broad skeletal structure and robust physique beneath. This physical presence radiates an aesthetic that serves as an expression of both realism and idealism. While the artisan has crystallized spiritual and human beauty within every stroke of the chisel, this beauty is by no means monolithic. It is multifaceted and fluid—a construct shaped by the artisan’s creative engagement, yet open to fresh interpretation by the viewer. Within the sculptural repertoire of Gandhara art, images of the Buddha and narratives of his life constitute the central thematic core. At a cursory glance, many Buddha statues may appear superficially similar—almost uniform in appearance; yet, upon pausing to examine them closely, one discovers that each sculpture harbors subtle distinctions: some exude gentle compassion, others appear solemn and majestic; some emphasize his human qualities, while others lean more toward the divine. These variations represent the distinct interpretations of the "ideal" held by different artisans—reflections of their own inner spirits and technical mastery. And I—standing before this sculpture as a viewer—am undergoing a transformation of my own: a moment of "holding up a mirror." The Buddha figure standing directly before me is handsome, graceful, serene, and remarkably lifelike. I recall the first time I encountered this sculpture; so absorbed was I in observing it that I instinctively—and unconsciously—adjusted my own attire. In that moment, I asked myself: Could I, too, possess such elegance? Or, more precisely: Could I be nourished by this "cultural ideal" and thereby become a better person? In that instant, aesthetic experience triggered an awakening of self-awareness; it was as if, reflected in the idealized image of "the Other," I caught a glimpse of the state of being I had yet to attain. As the exhibition drew near and I needed to draft accompanying text, I once again paused before this sculpture, tracing its contours and sensing the aura it exuded. Yet this time, my perception of that "ideal" had shifted. To some, my focus on this occasion might have seemed slightly peculiar; yet therein lies the true allure of a work of art that rewards sustained contemplation—it invariably inspires fresh thoughts and feelings. In this latest moment of "holding up a mirror," I found my anxieties soothed by—of all things—the Buddha’s gently rounded belly. It dawned on me: even such a sublime, idealized figure makes no attempt to conceal the authentic, natural reality of the human body. I could not help but reflect upon the historical ways in which society has disciplined the body through the lens of "aesthetics." This form of control is not exercised through brute force, but rather proceeds insidiously—through aesthetic tastes, media portrayals, and so-called standards of beauty—leading individuals to engage in self-disciplining acts under the illusion of making free choices. In our contemporary society, the elite class pursues sculpted physiques, vegan diets, and meticulous grooming—practices that serve, at their core, as signifiers of their purchasing power. Conversely, the bodies of the vast majority of ordinary people are frequently stigmatized—labeled as "fat," "dark-skinned," "sloppy," or "unfashionable." These labels not only expose deep-seated class prejudices but also compel many individuals—even at the cost of their own health and by suppressing their natural selves—to conform to these rigid "standards of beauty." This constitutes a form of aesthetic hegemony: a system that employs a singular standard to suppress the possibility of other forms of beauty, thereby manufacturing widespread body anxiety and a profound sense of self-alienation—a fate from which I, too, was once not exempt. In a consumerist society, beauty is no longer merely a sensory experience; it has been transmuted into a consumer objective and a commodity governed by market logic. Beauty has evolved into a performance of power structures: the closer a human body aligns with mainstream aesthetic norms, the more readily it garners cultural validation and social resources. We have gradually lost sight of the true essence of beauty—and how, exactly, one ought to interpret a person’s "truly beautiful form." In *Body Aesthetics*, Richard Shusterman observes: "The body is not merely a tool for displaying beauty, but rather the core medium through which beauty is experienced, expressed, and generated." Beauty is not confined to mere external harmony; rather, it is a holistic allure radiating from an individual’s inner psyche, spiritual disposition, and emotional energy. It is neither fixed within a specific concrete form nor contingent upon external standards of judgment; instead, it serves as the palpable manifestation of a state of mind. It is a dynamic, generative state—one that shifts and evolves in tandem with the flow of time, emotion, circumstance, and consciousness. It is not about "becoming a certain way," but rather about "being in a certain way." This is akin to how, when we discuss the beauty of a Gandhara sculpture, we are not truly speaking of the perfection of its physical form; rather, we are speaking of how it conveys—through non-verbal means—an aura of serenity, peace, wisdom, and inclusiveness. This aura is palpable, moving, and wholesome. Yet, within a cultural landscape shaped jointly by social media and capital, aesthetic standards have become increasingly monolithic. The true essence of beauty ought to be pluralistic, open-ended, and fluid—defined by its state rather than its form. As I write this, I am suddenly reminded of a scene from a visit to the Louvre in Paris: an art guide, speaking with palpable passion, was introducing the museum’s sculptures to a tour group. As I happened to pass by, I overheard him say: "This—*this*—is the most beautiful form a woman can take." Intrigued, I turned to look; before me stood a Roman-era marble sculpture of a female nude. Her figure was by no means slender; her arms, waist, and hips bore the soft curves of natural flesh—what we today would label a "pear-shaped" figure. Yet, that very sense of poise and authenticity moved me far more deeply than the "standardized beauty" so prevalent in our current age. We often say that beauty is something to be felt, not calculated. But we seem to have forgotten a fundamental truth: the starting point for *all* feeling is the body itself. Ultimately, the state of our body has long since determined the very manner in which we engage with the world. A weary body naturally struggles to radiate a luminous aura; conversely, a relaxed and serene body—without the need for a single spoken word—conveys a power born of stillness and wisdom. When we encounter the smile of a middle-aged person—even if their face bears the traces of wrinkles—and are moved by the vastness and warmth of their inner spirit, we witness a charm that defies quantification by "standard aesthetics." This lies at the very heart of the truth that "beauty is not merely physical form, but a state of being." Thus, as I stand before this Gandhara sculpture, I perceive not only the beauty of the artwork itself but, more profoundly, the possibility of a fresh understanding of what "beauty" truly means. It shatters my illusions of perfectionism, allowing me to experience a state of being that is authentic, healthy, and natural—and, in doing so, prompts me to reflect upon the oppressive pressures that contemporary society places upon the human body. Ideals should not stand in opposition to reality; rather, they should serve as the starting point from which we come to understand ourselves, embrace others, and appreciate the world around us. Beauty lies not in "what one becomes," but in the effortless grace of "being exactly as one is."

excerpt.en: A meditating Shakyamuni in schist, severe in outline and serene in presence.

viewingNote.zh: 犍陀罗，一个遥远而闪亮的地名，于空间意义上位于现今巴基斯坦北部与阿 富汗东部的交界处。那里曾是丝绸之路的重要节点之一，是东西方文化的交汇 点。“希腊风格的佛教艺术” 是艺术史对犍陀罗的命名，这个命名的内蕴充满着 文化与文明的冲撞与融合，也许是造像的艺术光芒太过耀眼以至于遮蔽起了侵略 的历史与流血的战争——那场遥远的亚历山大东征。 公元前 4 世纪， 亚历山大大帝在灭亡波斯、征服中亚之后， 来到了犍陀罗地 区， 在这里建立了一处希腊殖民地——巴克特里亚王国，也就是今天的阿富汗和 巴基斯坦地区。公元前 323 年，亚历山大大帝病逝于巴比伦，他的部将在战争和 炮火之后利用工匠、艺术，抚慰着崩塌的文明，东西方文化的碰撞、相融开始了。 直到公元前 145 年，巴克特里亚被大月氏人和部分塞种人所征服。此后，残存于 自喀布尔谷地至旁遮普地区的巴克特里亚——希腊人分裂为数个小王国，相互征 伐不已。在公元前 1 世纪末至公元 1 世纪初，随着塞种人和大月氏人势力的进一 步推进，巴克特里亚王国的历史从此结束。那时正处于青春期的佛教造像艺术 和艺术正处于萌芽期的贵霜帝国便立刻吸收了这股强大的美学力量。于是便有 了我们现在看到的犍陀罗艺术。 眼前的犍陀罗释迦牟尼坐像穿越了枪弹炮火、经受住了一千七百多年的沧桑 磨灭，出现在了一个互联网高速发展的时代。它带着那个时代人们的骄傲、虔 诚、智慧穿过了战乱、苦难和荒诞，我们还能有幸遇见，何其不易。在希腊的美 学中，雕塑就是建筑，追求空间。雕塑与建筑没有办法分割，浮雕的塑造也同样 遵循着建筑的空间感。在建筑上的大量圆雕与浮雕，它们装饰着建筑本身，并承 载一些结构的功能。同样，我们看到的犍陀罗艺术也是在对空间、建筑理解的基 础上，融合了东方佛教题材的共同呈现。 佛陀双手结禅定印作为故事的主体人物出现在整个故事的叙事中。佛陀脑后 是圆形的头光，脸庞年轻英俊，身形健壮、结实。弧形的眉毛之间为圆形白 毫，深邃的眼窝眼神柔和，微笑的嘴角上为象征贵族的八字胡，这种写实化的塑 造，配上立体的眉骨与直挺的鼻型，当站在不同角度观看时，会感受到一种俊 美、松弛、平和的人物气质；看似毫无波澜的面部表情，似乎在诉说的着无尽情感，又似有些淡淡忧伤，温柔、生动的人物状态在匠人细腻的刻画下变得鲜活而 具体。佛陀身着象征印度王公贵族的装束，袒露右肩、头戴敷巾冠、耳饰、臂 钏、手镯以及华丽的多重项链。右肩横跨着由三根串成一条的梵线，梵线是印度 本土文化中成人礼仪式上老师授予的象征物。腰间横跨圣索，绳索上点缀有大 小、款式不一的宝盒。下身穿着由一片式布料包裹成型的印度传统裙裤。质感 轻柔的布匹垂落于莲台前方，折出一条一条的 U 型纹理，与左臂的服饰线条上下 呼应，刀刻斧凿间的线条写实、流畅、有力。 宽大舒展的莲瓣最右侧为带有头光的协侍菩萨，协侍菩萨同样佩戴有象征贵 族身份的敷巾冠，身着传统服饰、佩戴珠宝项链，以自在坐的坐姿虔诚注视、端 详着佛陀。尽管扶靠的右手已断，但那扭头侧耳的神态中充满向往、满是崇敬。 旁边同样带头光的协侍菩萨正在合掌礼敬。作为圣鸟的桓娑，三只依次有序的站 立在莲台下方，它们有的扭头向外、有的凝视前方，在祥和氛围的笼罩下非常自 在，桓娑们没有任何一丝的紧张、惊恐。桓娑的出现生动的凸显了万物有灵，也 多了一种自然和谐之美。另外也值得一提的是在犍陀罗的雕塑中，桓娑出现在佛 陀禅思入定的场景中的构图是既特别又少见的。整件雕塑比例把控精准，应该出 自一位非常成熟的匠人之手，在旁边胁侍菩萨尺寸的烘托下，佛陀的庄严和神性 得到了放大，显得无比高大。在尺寸大小、构图的对比下，整体视觉效果大于本 身 70 厘米的高度，在没看见实物前，视觉错觉告诉我这件犍陀罗的高度也许和 一位成年人身高等比。 这件雕塑在人物比例排布以及细节的刻画中，体现了匠人对理想化的精准把 握以及对宗教情感的深刻理解。让我们能更轻易地在雕塑身上，感受到一种平 和与伟岸、佛性与静穆。在那个破碎又璀璨的时代，它被创作出来了，现在我们 看到的它是残缺的，但于我而言，它又是完整的。在月升日落中它见证了朝代的 更迭、频繁的战争、不尽的牺牲与流血。而在这场文明的冲撞与融合中，带来的 精神与美学力量却是无止尽的。在这出文明的悲喜剧中孕育出的犍陀罗文化，也 成为了艺术史上一颗璀璨的遗珠。
viewingNote.en: Gandhara—a distant yet luminous name—is situated geographically at the crossroads of present-day northern Pakistan and eastern Afghanistan. It once served as a vital nexus of the Silk Road and a pivotal meeting point for Eastern and Western cultures. "Greco-Buddhist Art" is the designation art history has bestowed upon Gandhara; inherent within this label lies a profound narrative of the collision and fusion of cultures and civilizations. Perhaps the artistic brilliance of its statuary was so dazzling that it overshadowed the underlying history of invasion and bloody warfare—specifically, the distant Eastern campaigns of Alexander the Great. In the 4th century BCE, having dismantled the Persian Empire and conquered Central Asia, Alexander the Great arrived in the Gandhara region. Here, he established a Greek colony—the Kingdom of Bactria—encompassing the territory that constitutes modern-day Afghanistan and Pakistan. In 323 BCE, Alexander the Great passed away in Babylon. In the aftermath of war and bombardment, his generals turned to artisans and the arts to soothe the wounds of a shattered civilization; thus began the collision and fusion of Eastern and Western cultures. This era persisted until 145 BCE, when Bactria was conquered by the Yuezhi and certain Saka tribes. Subsequently, the surviving Greco-Bactrian communities—scattered across the region stretching from the Kabul Valley to the Punjab—fragmented into numerous small kingdoms, locked in ceaseless internecine warfare. Between the late 1st century BCE and the early 1st century CE, as the influence of the Saka and Yuezhi peoples continued to expand, the history of the Bactrian Kingdom came to a definitive end. At that very juncture—when the art of Buddhist iconography was in its formative "adolescence" and the Kushan Empire was just beginning to take root—both immediately absorbed this potent aesthetic force. The result is the Gandhara art we behold today. The seated statue of Shakyamuni Buddha from Gandhara that stands before us has traversed a landscape of gunfire and artillery, enduring the vicissitudes of over seventeen centuries of wear and tear, to emerge in this era of rapid internet advancement. Bearing the pride, piety, and wisdom of the people of that distant age, it has journeyed through epochs of war, suffering, and absurdity; that we are still privileged enough to encounter it today is, indeed, a rare and precious gift. In the aesthetics of ancient Greece, sculpture was synonymous with architecture, driven by a pursuit of spatiality. Sculpture and architecture were inseparable; likewise, the creation of relief carvings adhered strictly to the spatial sensibilities inherent in architecture. The numerous examples of *in-the-round* sculpture and relief carvings found on architectural structures served not only to adorn the buildings themselves but also to fulfill certain structural functions. Similarly, Gandhara art—as we observe it today—represents a synthesis of Eastern Buddhist iconography and artistic forms, grounded in a profound understanding of space and architecture. The Buddha, depicted with his hands forming the *Dhyana Mudra* (meditation gesture), stands as the central figure within the narrative of the entire composition. Behind the Buddha’s head radiates a circular halo; his face is youthful and handsome, while his physique appears robust and sturdy. A circular *urna* (tuft of hair) marks the space between his arched eyebrows; his deep-set eyes convey a gentle gaze, and a mustache—styled in a manner symbolizing aristocratic status—rests above the corners of his smiling lips. This highly realistic sculptural style—characterized by prominent brow ridges and a straight, aquiline nose—evokes a distinct aura of handsomeness, serenity, and tranquility when viewed from various angles. His seemingly placid facial expression appears to articulate a wealth of unspoken emotion—perhaps even a hint of subtle melancholy—as the Buddha’s gentle yet vivid presence is brought to life through the craftsman’s meticulous artistry. The Buddha is clad in attire emblematic of Indian royalty and nobility: his right shoulder is left bare, while his head is adorned with a turban-like headcloth; he wears earrings, armbands, bracelets, and a magnificent multi-stranded necklace. Traversing his right shoulder is a *sacred thread* (Brahminical cord)—composed of three strands twisted together—a symbol bestowed by a teacher during the coming-of-age rituals of indigenous Indian culture. A sacred sash crosses his waist, studded with jewel-boxes of varying sizes and designs. His lower body is draped in a traditional Indian lower garment, formed from a single piece of fabric wrapped and pleated around the figure. The soft, flowing fabric cascades down in front of the lotus pedestal, forming a series of U-shaped folds that visually echo the lines of the drapery on his left arm; the lines—etched with the precision of a sculptor’s chisel—are at once realistic, fluid, and powerful. Flanking the outermost edge of the broad, gracefully unfurling lotus petals stands an attendant Bodhisattva, distinguished by a radiant halo. This attendant, too, wears a turban—a symbol of aristocratic status—and is clad in traditional attire adorned with jeweled necklaces. Seated in a posture of relaxed ease, the Bodhisattva gazes upon the Buddha with profound piety and deep contemplation. Although the right arm, which once served as a support, has been broken off, the tilt of the head and the inclination of the ear convey an expression brimming with yearning and reverence. Adjacent to this figure stands another attendant Bodhisattva—likewise crowned with a halo—with hands joined in a gesture of respectful salutation. Beneath the lotus pedestal stand three *Hamsa* (sacred geese), arranged in an orderly sequence; some turn their heads outward, while others gaze straight ahead. Enveloped by an atmosphere of auspicious tranquility, the *Hamsas* appear utterly at ease, betraying not the slightest trace of tension or alarm.
